ingredients.name: Vitamin A
ingredients.notes: A preformed Vitamin A ester, similar to retinyl palmitate. Absorbs at 70–90% with no conversion needed. Slightly less common than palmitate but equally effective for correcting deficiency.

condition_hits.mechanism: Excess preformed vitamin A intake in pregnancy is associated with teratogenic risk.
condition_hits.action: Avoid high-dose preformed vitamin A in pregnancy unless clinician-directed.
condition_hits.alert_headline: Keep preformed vitamin A within prenatal range
condition_hits.alert_body: Preformed vitamin A (retinol) is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; beta-carotene is not. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total preformed retinol intake from all sources and talk to your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Preformed retinol is dose-sensitive — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_hits.mechanism: Retinol (preformed vitamin A) is stored in hepatic stellate cells (Ito cells) as retinyl esters. Chronic liver disease impairs retinol clearance and stellate cell function, causing accumulation even at doses below standard UL. Hypervitaminosis A induces stellate cell hypertrophy and proliferation, leading to progressive fibrosis, non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, and eventual cirrhosis. CYP2E1 induction (common in alcoholic liver disease) enhances retinol hepatotoxicity via microsomal oxidative activation. Clinically relevant injury has been reported at supplemental doses ≥25,000 IU/day in individuals with underlying liver disease.
condition_hits.action: Limit supplemental preformed vitamin A (retinol/retinyl esters) to ≤5,000 IU/day in patients with liver disease. Beta-carotene is preferred. Monitor LFTs and clinical signs of hypervitaminosis (hepatomegaly, ascites). Avoid multivitamins with high retinol doses.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin A is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Vitamin A.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin A depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_hits.mechanism: Dose and form dependent risk is primarily tied to preformed retinoids.
condition_hits.action: Applies to preformed vitamin A forms; carotenoids are not handled by this threshold rule.

ingredients.name: Vitamin C
ingredients.notes: The standard, most widely studied form of vitamin C. Highly bioavailable at normal doses (200 mg: ~90% absorbed). Acidic — may cause stomach upset in sensitive individuals at high doses. The benchmark against which all other forms are compared.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin C is essential, but excessive supplemental intake above UL is not recommended in pregnancy.
condition_hits.action: Keep vitamin C intake within guideline ranges; avoid high-dose supplementation without clinical need.
condition_hits.alert_headline: Keep vitamin C within prenatal range
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin C is required in pregnancy, but intakes above the upper limit can raise tolerability concerns. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin C is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

condition_hits.mechanism: Dose-dependent tolerability; excessive daily intake can increase adverse-effect risk.
condition_hits.action: Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.

ingredients.name: Vitamin D
ingredients.notes: The gold standard form of vitamin D — identical to what your skin produces from sunlight. D3 raises and maintains blood 25(OH)D levels significantly better than D2. The most studied and recommended form by clinical guidelines worldwide.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D does not directly affect platelet aggregation at physiological or supplemental doses. However, high-dose vitamin D supplementation may interact with warfarin sensitivity in patients with concurrent vitamin K deficiency by affecting the vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X). The vitamin D endocrine system and vitamin K share overlapping roles in gamma-carboxylation of hepatic Gla-proteins.
condition_hits.action: Vitamin D at standard supplemental doses (up to 4000 IU/day) is not a clinically significant bleeding risk on its own. Monitor if taking high-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) with anticoagulants.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D exerts broad immunomodulatory effects, generally shifting immune responses toward tolerance via Treg cell upregulation and Th17 suppression. Low vitamin D status is associated with increased autoimmune risk. Supplementation may be beneficial in Hashimoto thyroiditis and multiple sclerosis, but high doses in active autoimmune disease require monitoring.
condition_hits.action: Vitamin D supplementation at standard doses (1000–4000 IU/day) is generally safe in autoimmune conditions. High-dose vitamin D requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, and moderate supplementation is generally considered beneficial. However, pharmacologic doses (>4000 IU/day chronically) can cause hypercalcemia, which promotes vascular calcification and may worsen coronary artery disease. The benefit-risk ratio is dose-dependent.
condition_hits.action: Target serum 25(OH)D levels of 30–60 ng/mL. Avoid high-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) without clinical monitoring of calcium and 25(OH)D levels.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D receptors (VDR) are expressed in ovarian granulosa cells, endometrium, and placenta. A 2017 review (Voulgaris et al., Hormones) summarized strong associations between vitamin D status and PCOS metabolic parameters, endometriosis, and IVF outcomes, with deficiency linked to lower clinical pregnancy rates. Meta-analyses of IVF studies suggest women with 25(OH)D >20 ng/mL have significantly better outcomes, though causality is not fully established.
condition_hits.action: Check 25(OH)D levels prior to or during TTC. Correct deficiency (<20 ng/mL) with supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) under clinician guidance. Achieving sufficiency (30–60 ng/mL) is recommended before and during early pregnancy. Standard prenatal vitamins provide ~400–1000 IU/day.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin D with your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: High-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) has been associated with altered warfarin sensitivity in some patients. The proposed mechanism involves shared metabolic pathways with vitamin K: vitamin D may modulate hepatic expression of vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X), and vitamin D deficiency itself affects coagulation factor synthesis. Vitamin D and K have documented synergistic effects on Gla-protein carboxylation, and disruption of this balance at supraphysiological vitamin D doses may affect INR stability in warfarin patients.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor INR stability when significantly changing vitamin D supplementation dose, particularly at high doses (>10,000 IU/day). Routine supplementation doses (1000-4000 IU/day) typically do not require INR monitoring changes.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: No established direct interaction between vitamin D supplementation and antiplatelet drug activity. Monitoring recommended at high doses due to broader hemostatic pathway involvement.
drug_class_hits.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses. Monitor for unusual bleeding if taking high-dose vitamin D with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: No established pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between vitamin D and NSAIDs on platelet or coagulation pathways. Theoretical concern at high doses given shared anti-inflammatory regulatory roles.
drug_class_hits.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses.
drug_class_hits.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D has additive immunomodulatory effects that may complement immunosuppressant therapy. Standard-dose supplementation is generally considered safe alongside immunosuppressants and may reduce corticosteroid-induced bone loss.
drug_class_hits.action: Standard vitamin D supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels annually. High-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) requires physician oversight.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

ingredients.name: Thiamine
ingredients.notes: The standard thiamine form used in most multivitamins and fortified foods. Very shelf-stable. Well-absorbed and effective for preventing and correcting B1 deficiency.

ingredients.name: Riboflavin
ingredients.notes: The standard form of vitamin B2 used in most supplements and fortified foods. Your body must convert it to the active forms FMN and FAD before use. Well-absorbed and effective for general B2 supplementation.

ingredients.name: Niacin
ingredients.notes: The non-flushing amide form of B3. Does not cause vasodilation. Preferred for skin health, cellular repair, and general B3 supplementation. Does NOT have the same lipid-lowering effects as nicotinic acid.

condition_hits.mechanism: At pharmacologic doses (>=1g/day), niacin increases hepatic glucose output and decreases insulin sensitivity. FPG increases of 4-5% and HbA1c increases up to 0.3% documented.
condition_hits.action: Can worsen glycemic control. Monitor glucose after initiating niacin or increasing dose. Low-dose niacin in multivitamins is not a concern.
condition_hits.alert_body: Niacin can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding Niacin and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_hits.informational_note: Niacin affects glycemic control — relevant to people with diabetes.

condition_hits.mechanism: Niacin at doses exceeding 2 g/day causes dose-dependent hepatotoxicity via impairment of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and calcium homeostasis disruption. Sustained-release formulations are associated with higher hepatotoxicity risk than immediate-release at equivalent doses. Case reports include acute fulminant hepatic failure requiring ICU admission. In patients with pre-existing liver disease, baseline impaired hepatic metabolism reduces the threshold for injury, and niacin-induced vasodilatation may also exacerbate portal hypertension.
condition_hits.action: Use with caution in liver disease; doses above 500 mg/day should require physician supervision and baseline LFTs. Avoid sustained-release niacin formulations in patients with liver disease. Discontinue if ALT/AST elevations exceed 3x ULN.
condition_hits.alert_body: Niacin is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Niacin.
condition_hits.informational_note: Niacin depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_hits.mechanism: Pharmacologic-dose niacin (≥1g/day) raises HDL-C and lowers triglycerides and LDL-C, but large RCTs (AIM-HIGH, HPS2-THRIVE) failed to show cardiovascular benefit when added to statin therapy, and HPS2-THRIVE found excess serious adverse events with niacin. Extended-release niacin combined with laropiprant was withdrawn in Europe. At doses ≥1g/day, niacin also impairs glucose tolerance.
condition_hits.action: High-dose niacin for cholesterol management has fallen out of favor due to lack of incremental cardiovascular benefit on top of statin therapy and significant side effects. Consult prescriber before initiating pharmacologic niacin for lipid management.
condition_hits.alert_body: Niacin can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding Niacin — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
condition_hits.informational_note: Niacin affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Niacin can raise blood glucose, potentially requiring adjustment of diabetes medication doses.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor glucose closely. Diabetes medication dose adjustment may be needed.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Niacin can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding Niacin and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Niacin affects glycemic control — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: High-dose niacin combined with statins increases the risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis beyond that of statin monotherapy. This risk is particularly elevated with gemfibrozil-statin combinations, but niacin-statin combinations also carry increased myopathy risk, estimated at approximately 0.12% incidence in combination therapy trials. The mechanism involves pharmacodynamic additive muscle toxicity.
drug_class_hits.action: If high-dose niacin (≥1g/day) is co-prescribed with statins, monitor for myopathy symptoms (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine). Obtain baseline and periodic CK levels. Avoid high-intensity statin doses in this combination.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Niacin shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding Niacin and watch for new muscle aches.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Niacin affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

ingredients.name: Vitamin B6
ingredients.notes: The most common synthetic B6 form in supplements and fortified foods. Requires liver conversion to active P5P, which can be inefficient in some individuals (especially those with liver conditions or certain genetic variants).

condition_hits.mechanism: High-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6) has been investigated as a pharmacologic agent to suppress postpartum lactation. At doses of 450–600 mg/day for 5–7 days, two trials found ~95% inhibition of lactation, though four other studies failed to demonstrate efficacy by prolactin measurement or clinical assessment. The proposed mechanism is dopaminergic: pyridoxine is a cofactor in dopamine synthesis; elevated dopamine tonically inhibits prolactin release from the anterior pituitary. A 1979 NEJM case report warned of dangers of vitamin B6 in nursing mothers at high doses. Normal multivitamin doses (1.9 mg/day recommended for lactation) are not a concern.
condition_hits.action: Standard prenatal vitamin doses of B6 are safe during lactation. High-dose B6 supplementation (>50 mg/day) during established breastfeeding may suppress milk production. Avoid high-dose B6 if breastfeeding is intended. Consult lactation specialist before use.
condition_hits.alert_headline: High-dose B6 may affect milk supply
condition_hits.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
condition_hits.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

condition_hits.mechanism: Normal doses of B6 are essential in pregnancy. High pharmacologic doses during lactation may suppress milk production via dopaminergic inhibition of prolactin.
condition_hits.action: This rule applies to supplemental/pharmacologic doses. Prenatal vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate and encouraged.

ingredients.name: Folic Acid
ingredients.notes: The synthetic, inactive form of folate used in most cheap supplements and fortified foods. Must be converted through multiple enzymatic steps to become active 5-MTHF. Up to 40% of people carry MTHFR variants that impair this conversion, potentially leading to unmetabolized folic acid buildup.

condition_hits.mechanism: Periconceptional folic acid/folate supplementation reduces neural tube defect (NTD) risk by 69% (RR 0.31, 95% CI 0.17–0.58) in Cochrane meta-analysis of 5 RCTs (6708 births). The protective effect operates during neural tube closure (days 21–28 post-conception), before many women know they are pregnant. Supplementation should therefore begin at least 1 month (ideally 3 months) before conception. For women with MTHFR polymorphisms, methylfolate (5-MTHF) is preferred as it bypasses the enzymatic conversion step.
condition_hits.action: Begin folate/folic acid supplementation at least 1 month before attempting conception. Standard dose: 400–800 mcg/day for low-risk women; 4 mg/day (4000 mcg) for women with prior NTD-affected pregnancy. Prefer methylfolate (5-MTHF) if MTHFR variant is known. Continue through first trimester.
condition_hits.alert_body: Folate used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Folate with your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Folate is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

condition_hits.mechanism: Folate is essential throughout pregnancy and lactation for maternal and fetal/infant cell synthesis. The TTC rule transitions to ongoing pregnancy/lactation supplementation guidance.
condition_hits.action: Folate supplementation started periconceptionally should continue throughout pregnancy as part of prenatal vitamin regimen.
condition_hits.alert_body: Folate started before pregnancy should continue through pregnancy and breastfeeding as part of prenatal support. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Folate is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

ingredients.name: Vitamin B12
ingredients.notes: The most common and cheapest synthetic B12 form. Contains a cyanide group that must be removed during metabolism (the amount is toxicologically insignificant). Requires liver conversion to active methylcobalamin or adenosylcobalamin. The most clinically studied form.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and one-carbon metabolism. B12 deficiency elevates homocysteine via impaired methionine synthase activity, and hyperhomocysteinemia is associated with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), implantation failure, and embryotoxicity. Case-control studies document significantly lower serum B12 in women with recurrent miscarriage vs. controls (mean 197 vs 300 pg/mL, p=0.004 in one Syrian cohort; 178 vs 269 pg/mL, p<0.001 in a Polish cohort). B12 is a standard component of preconception care and prenatal vitamins. Optimizing B12 status before conception is associated with improved fertility outcomes and reduced RPL risk via homocysteine normalization.
condition_hits.action: Ensure adequate vitamin B12 status (serum B12 >400 pg/mL is optimal preconceptionally) when trying to conceive. Standard preconception supplementation: 2.4–6 mcg/day (adequate intake) up to higher doses if deficient. B12 supplementation is recommended as part of a comprehensive preconception vitamin regimen for all women TTC, and is particularly important for vegans, vegetarians, those with gastric conditions, or those on metformin (which reduces B12 absorption).
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin B12 used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin B12 with your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin B12 is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

condition_hits.mechanism: B12 is an essential nutrient throughout pregnancy and lactation. Deficiency is associated with neural tube defects, developmental delay, and recurrent pregnancy loss via hyperhomocysteinemia. Standard prenatal vitamins contain B12; supplementation should continue throughout pregnancy and during breastfeeding.
condition_hits.action: B12 supplementation during pregnancy is part of standard prenatal care. Vegans and those with malabsorption (pernicious anemia, gastric surgery, metformin use) need higher supplemental doses.
condition_hits.alert_body: B12 is essential throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding, and supplementation started pre-conception should continue. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
condition_hits.informational_note: B12 is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

ingredients.name: Biotin
ingredients.notes: The natural, biologically active form of biotin (the D-enantiomer). This is what your body actually uses for metabolizing fats, carbohydrates, and proteins. The vast majority of commercial biotin supplements contain d-biotin. Top-tier B7 form.

condition_hits.mechanism: High-dose biotin (>5 mg/day, common in hair/nail supplements) interferes with biotin-streptavidin immunoassay platforms used for TSH, free T4, free T3, and thyroglobulin measurement. Causes false-low TSH and false-high free thyroid hormone results, mimicking hyperthyroidism. Does not alter actual thyroid function.
condition_hits.action: Stop biotin supplements at least 2 days before thyroid function testing to prevent assay interference. Inform your healthcare provider about biotin use if thyroid tests are planned.
condition_hits.alert_body: Biotin can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding Biotin and monitor your labs.
condition_hits.informational_note: Biotin affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

ingredients.name: Pantothenic Acid
ingredients.notes: The most common B5 supplement form — calcium salt of pantothenic acid. Very shelf-stable, well-absorbed, and widely used in multivitamins and B-complex formulas. Provides ~92% pantothenic acid by weight.

ingredients.name: Calcium
ingredients.notes: The most widely used and cheapest calcium form (~40% elemental calcium). REQUIRES stomach acid for absorption — must be taken with food. Poorly absorbed in people with low stomach acid (common in adults over 50). Can cause gas, bloating, and constipation. The form in Tums and most budget supplements.

condition_hits.mechanism: Calcium supplements (not dietary calcium) are associated with a modestly increased risk of myocardial infarction. Bolland et al. WHI reanalysis found RR 1.24 (95% CI 1.07–1.45) for MI with supplemental calcium. Proposed mechanism: acute hypercalcemia following bolus supplementation may promote coronary arterial calcification and thrombosis. Risk may be attenuated by co-administration with vitamin D.
condition_hits.action: In individuals with existing heart disease, prefer dietary calcium over supplements. If supplementation is necessary, use the lowest effective dose and split doses to blunt calcium spikes. Discuss with cardiologist.
condition_hits.alert_body: Calcium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding calcium.
condition_hits.informational_note: Calcium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Calcium forms insoluble chelate complexes with levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing oral absorption by 20–25%. Effect is dose-dependent and clinically significant at standard supplementation doses (600–1000 mg/day).
drug_class_hits.action: Separate calcium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours to prevent absorption interference.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Calcium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing calcium away from your dose.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Calcium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

ingredients.name: Iron
ingredients.notes: A well-absorbed iron salt with 33% elemental iron content — the highest of the common ferrous salts. Commonly used in prenatal vitamins and iron-deficiency treatment. Can cause GI side effects (constipation, nausea) but less than ferrous sulfate at equivalent doses.

condition_hits.mechanism: The liver is the principal organ of iron storage and regulation. In liver disease, hepatic hepcidin production is impaired, disrupting iron homeostasis and increasing intestinal iron absorption. Iron supplementation in this context risks exacerbating hepatic iron overload, which drives oxidative stress via the Fenton reaction (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ + •OH), lipid peroxidation, and progressive liver fibrosis. This mechanism is most critical in hereditary hemochromatosis and alcoholic liver disease, where iron accumulation is already elevated, but applies broadly to chronic liver disease including NAFLD/NASH, hepatitis C, and cirrhosis.
condition_hits.action: Avoid routine iron supplementation in liver disease patients unless iron deficiency anemia is confirmed by labs (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC). Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Monitor ferritin and transferrin saturation regularly.
condition_hits.alert_body: Iron is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of iron.
condition_hits.informational_note: Iron depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Iron chelates with methyldopa and certain ACE inhibitors (captopril) in the GI tract, reducing drug absorption and bioavailability.
drug_class_hits.action: Separate iron supplement from methyldopa or captopril by at least 2 hours.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Iron can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding iron.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Iron can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Ferrous sulfate and other iron salts form stable insoluble complexes with levothyroxine, reducing thyroid hormone absorption. Concurrent ingestion can significantly impair replacement therapy efficacy.
drug_class_hits.action: Separate iron supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Iron can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing iron away from your dose.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Iron affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

ingredients.name: Iodine
ingredients.notes: Natural iodine from kelp seaweed (Laminaria, Ascophyllum, or bladderwrack species). Provides iodine within a whole-food matrix alongside trace minerals. The downside: iodine content varies wildly between batches (50-500%), making precise dosing difficult. A good natural option but less predictable than potassium iodide.

condition_hits.mechanism: Iodine is essential in pregnancy, but excessive intake can adversely affect maternal/fetal thyroid function.
condition_hits.action: Keep total iodine intake within guideline ranges; avoid excess supplementation.
condition_hits.alert_body: Iodine is required during pregnancy, but excess can affect maternal and fetal thyroid function. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total iodine intake and talk to your clinician if in doubt.
condition_hits.informational_note: Iodine is dose-sensitive — both deficiency and excess matter for pregnancy and lactation.

condition_hits.mechanism: Excess iodine triggers the Wolff-Chaikoff effect, acutely suppressing thyroid hormone synthesis. Individuals with pre-existing thyroid disease (Hashimoto thyroiditis, Graves disease) are particularly susceptible to iodine-induced hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. Kelp/seaweed products contain highly variable iodine loads (50–500% of stated dose) and carry additional risk.
condition_hits.action: Avoid high-dose iodine or kelp/seaweed supplements in thyroid disorders without physician oversight. Routine supplementation above the RDA (150 mcg/day) is not recommended.
condition_hits.alert_body: Iodine can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding iodine and monitor your labs.
condition_hits.informational_note: Iodine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

ingredients.name: Magnesium
ingredients.notes: The cheapest and most common magnesium form in budget supplements — but one of the worst absorbed (only ~4% bioavailability). Its high elemental magnesium content (60%) is misleading because so little is actually absorbed. Primarily acts as an osmotic laxative. Avoid if you need to raise magnesium levels.

condition_hits.mechanism: Magnesium acts as a natural calcium channel blocker, producing modest BP reductions (2-4 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with pharmaceutical calcium channel blockers may enhance hypotensive action.
condition_hits.action: Generally safe at recommended doses. Inform prescriber if supplementing alongside calcium channel blockers. Monitor for dizziness.
condition_hits.alert_body: Magnesium can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding magnesium and monitor your readings.
condition_hits.informational_note: Magnesium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_hits.mechanism: Magnesium plays a critical role in cardiac electrophysiology, stabilizing cardiac membranes and modulating ion channels. Hypomagnesemia is associated with cardiac arrhythmias. However, in patients with heart disease who also have renal impairment (common comorbidity), magnesium supplements can accumulate and cause hypermagnesemia, leading to bradycardia, cardiac conduction block, and potentially cardiac arrest.
condition_hits.action: Use magnesium supplements with caution in heart disease patients, especially those with reduced kidney function. Do not exceed recommended doses without renal function assessment. Monitor for signs of hypermagnesemia (weakness, bradycardia).
condition_hits.alert_body: Magnesium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding magnesium.
condition_hits.informational_note: Magnesium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_hits.mechanism: Impaired renal magnesium excretion in CKD (especially eGFR <30) leads to hypermagnesemia accumulation. Supplemental magnesium doses safe in healthy adults can cause life-threatening hypermagnesemia in CKD patients (cardiac arrest, respiratory failure).
condition_hits.action: Avoid magnesium supplementation in severe CKD (eGFR <30) without nephrologist guidance. In mild-moderate CKD, use with caution and monitor serum Mg levels.
condition_hits.alert_body: In advanced kidney disease (eGFR below 30), supplemental magnesium can build up to risky levels. If you have CKD, talk to your nephrologist before adding magnesium.
condition_hits.informational_note: Magnesium excretion depends on kidney function — relevant to people with kidney disease.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Additive blood pressure lowering from natural calcium channel blocking activity.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor BP when supplementing alongside antihypertensives.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Magnesium can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding magnesium.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Magnesium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Divalent magnesium cations can bind levothyroxine and impair gastrointestinal absorption, similar to other divalent mineral-levothyroxine chelation interactions.
drug_class_hits.action: Separate magnesium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Magnesium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing magnesium away from your dose.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Magnesium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

ingredients.name: Zinc
ingredients.notes: The cheapest zinc form with the worst absorption. Despite containing 80% elemental zinc by weight, very little is actually bioavailable due to poor solubility. If your supplement uses zinc oxide, you're getting far less zinc than the label suggests. Avoid for zinc supplementation — it's chosen for cost, not efficacy.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Zinc chelation reduces levothyroxine absorption. Zinc is also required for thyroid hormone synthesis; deficiency can cause hypothyroidism, creating a complex bidirectional relationship.
drug_class_hits.action: Separate zinc supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Zinc can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing zinc away from your dose.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Zinc affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

ingredients.name: Selenium
ingredients.notes: The premium organic selenium form — an amino acid (methionine) with selenium replacing sulfur. Your body incorporates it directly into proteins, creating a selenium reserve that's released as needed. ~90% absorbed. The form used in the landmark SELECT and NPC cancer prevention trials.

condition_hits.mechanism: Selenium is essential for iodothyronine deiodinase (type I/II/III) function required to convert T4 to active T3. Selenium supplementation may benefit Hashimoto thyroiditis by reducing thyroid peroxidase antibody titers. However, excess selenium (>400 mcg/day) can paradoxically impair thyroid function and is toxic.
condition_hits.action: Selenium supplementation in thyroid disorders should be guided by serum selenium levels. Doses above 200 mcg/day should only be used under medical supervision.
condition_hits.alert_body: Selenium can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding selenium and monitor your labs.
condition_hits.informational_note: Selenium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

ingredients.name: Copper
ingredients.notes: Copper oxide is nearly insoluble in stomach acid — Baker (1999) showed it delivers almost no bioavailable copper. Despite this, it appears in many cheap multivitamins because it's shelf-stable and inexpensive. Essentially useless as a copper source. If your supplement lists copper oxide, the copper on the label is not meaningfully absorbed.

condition_hits.mechanism: Copper homeostasis depends on hepatic biliary excretion as the primary elimination route. In Wilson's disease, ATP7B mutations block copper export into bile, causing pathological hepatic accumulation leading to cirrhosis. In non-Wilson's liver disease, impaired biliary function reduces copper clearance, and supplemental copper can accumulate to hepatotoxic levels even at low doses (>2 mg/day).
condition_hits.action: Avoid copper supplementation above 2 mg/day in liver disease. In Wilson's disease, supplemental copper is contraindicated. Consult hepatologist before copper supplementation in chronic liver disease.
condition_hits.alert_body: Copper is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of copper.
condition_hits.informational_note: Copper depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

ingredients.name: Manganese
ingredients.notes: An inorganic salt form with lower absorption than organic chelates. More likely to cause GI upset at higher doses. Inexpensive, so it's common in budget multivitamins. If your label says sulfate, you're getting a basic form.

condition_hits.mechanism: Manganese is excreted almost exclusively via bile. Liver disease impairs biliary excretion, leading to systemic accumulation. Excess manganese deposits in the basal ganglia (globus pallidus), producing manganism — a Parkinson-like neurotoxic syndrome with rigidity, bradykinesia, and psychiatric symptoms. MRI T1 hyperintensity in liver disease patients with elevated manganese is well-documented.
condition_hits.action: Avoid manganese supplementation above 5 mg/day in liver disease. In advanced cirrhosis or liver failure, avoid supplemental manganese entirely. Evaluate manganese status if neurological symptoms develop.
condition_hits.alert_body: Manganese is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of manganese.
condition_hits.informational_note: Manganese depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

ingredients.name: Chromium
ingredients.notes: The label doesn't specify which chromium form is used. This gets a conservative score because it could be anything from cheap chromium chloride (very poorly absorbed) to premium picolinate. Look for the specific form name on your label for a more accurate score.

condition_hits.mechanism: Chromium can alter glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity.
condition_hits.action: Use with glucose monitoring if on diabetes treatment.
condition_hits.alert_body: Chromium can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding chromium and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_hits.informational_note: Chromium affects glycemic control — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_hits.action: Adjust medication only under clinician supervision.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Chromium can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding chromium and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Chromium affects glycemic control — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Chromium picolinate can reduce levothyroxine absorption when taken concurrently. Divalent chromium binds to thyroid hormone molecules in the gastrointestinal tract.
drug_class_hits.action: Separate chromium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Chromium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing chromium away from your dose.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Chromium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

ingredients.name: Molybdenum
ingredients.notes: The standard inorganic salt form and the most common molybdenum in multivitamins. Turnlund 1995 (the only published human isotope study) showed 88-93% absorption across all intake levels — making it highly bioavailable. Affordable and effective. The actual peer-reviewed absorption data comes from this form, not chelates.

ingredients.name: Choline
ingredients.notes: Budget choline salt — 41% choline by weight (highest elemental yield). Plasma choline AUC equivalent to Alpha-GPC and other forms (Stoll 2021 crossover). Limited BBB penetration vs Alpha-GPC/citicoline.

ingredients.name: Alpha-Lipoic Acid
ingredients.notes: Racemic (50:50 R+S) alpha-lipoic acid, the most common and cheapest form. Breithaupt-Grogler 1999: absolute bioavailability ~30% overall (R-component 38%, S-component 28%). Linus Pauling Institute review: 30-40%. Short half-life ~0.3-0.4 hours. Keith 2012 (PMC4084596): in young adults, racemic ALA actually showed higher AUC (1,772 ng-h/mL) than pure R-ALA (1,070 ng-h/mL). Thioctic acid is the pharmaceutical name.

condition_hits.mechanism: Enhances insulin-stimulated glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocation and reduces oxidative stress-mediated insulin resistance. May potentiate glucose-lowering effects of diabetes medications.
condition_hits.action: Monitor blood glucose when initiating ALA at doses >= 600 mg/day alongside diabetes medications.
condition_hits.alert_body: Alpha-lipoic acid can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding Alpha-lipoic acid and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_hits.informational_note: Alpha-lipoic acid affects glycemic control — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Additive insulin-sensitizing effect may increase hypoglycemia risk, especially with insulin and sulfonylureas.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor glucose and adjust medication only under clinician supervision.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Alpha-lipoic acid can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding Alpha-lipoic acid and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Alpha-lipoic acid affects glycemic control — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

ingredients.name: Inositol
ingredients.notes: The most abundant naturally occurring inositol isomer — your body makes it and it's found in fruits, beans, and grains. Myo-inositol is a second messenger in insulin signaling and plays a critical role in ovarian follicle maturation. Strong clinical evidence for PCOS (improves ovulation and insulin sensitivity at 2–4 g/day), anxiety, and OCD (up to 18 g/day). Often combined with D-chiro-inositol in a 40:1 ratio to mimic the body's natural balance.

condition_hits.mechanism: Myo-inositol (the primary isomer in supplements) is a second messenger in insulin signaling and plays a critical role in ovarian follicle maturation. In PCOS, insulin resistance disrupts the inositol phosphoglycan mediator system. Supplementation at 2–4 g/day myo-inositol (alone or in 40:1 ratio with D-chiro-inositol) improves ovulation rates, menstrual regularity, and fertility outcomes. An RCT (Agrawal et al. 2019) found significantly higher live birth rates with metformin + myo-inositol (55%) vs. metformin alone (26.67%, p=0.002) in PCOS women undergoing ovulation induction.
condition_hits.action: Myo-inositol (2–4 g/day) is a well-supported supplement for women with PCOS who are TTC. It improves ovulation and insulin sensitivity. Safe for TTC use. Also safe to continue in early pregnancy under clinician guidance. Not specifically indicated for non-PCOS TTC without ovulation issues.
condition_hits.alert_body: Inositol used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of inositol with your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

condition_hits.mechanism: Myo-inositol is a naturally occurring compound and generally considered safe in pregnancy. Some evidence supports its use to reduce gestational diabetes risk in PCOS pregnancies. Standard monitoring is appropriate.
condition_hits.action: Transition to clinician-supervised use once pregnancy is confirmed if continuing supplementation.
condition_hits.alert_body: Myo-inositol is generally considered safe in pregnancy, and use may continue under clinician supervision. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician supervision — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

ingredients.name: Amylase
ingredients.notes: Targeted individual enzymes (protease, lipase, amylase, bromelain, papain, serrapeptase, lactase, cellulase, etc.) designed to break down specific food components in the GI lumen. Enzymes work locally — systemic absorption is NOT the relevant metric; GI stability and enzyme activity delivery matter. Without enteric coating, many enzymes are partially denatured by stomach acid (~30-50% survival). OTC enzyme supplements are not FDA-regulated and enzyme concentrations are not guaranteed (Johns Hopkins Medicine). Well-characterized branded forms (DigeZyme, ProHydrolase) have more reliable potency.

ingredients.name: Green Tea leaf extract
ingredients.notes: Standardized green tea extract with ≥50% EGCG. EGCG Cmax 326 ng/mL after 4.5g green tea solids (PMID:9568793, Yang 1998). t1/2 5.0-5.5h. Chronic dosing (800mg/day x 4wks) increases AUC >60% suggesting conjugation pathway saturation (PMID:12960117, Chow 2003). Standard form for clinical use.

condition_hits.mechanism: Green tea catechins (EGCG) inhibit intestinal OATP transporters, reducing oral bioavailability of nadolol and potentially other beta-blockers, diminishing BP control.
condition_hits.action: Avoid taking green tea extract supplements close in time to beta-blockers. Separate doses by several hours.
condition_hits.alert_body: Green tea extract can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding green tea extract and monitor your readings.
condition_hits.informational_note: Green tea extract can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_hits.mechanism: EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), the principal catechin in green tea extract, undergoes hepatic first-pass metabolism. At supplemental doses (≥800 mg EGCG/day), EGCG saturates first-pass elimination and reaches hepatotoxic concentrations, inducing mitochondrial oxidative stress, hepatocellular pattern of liver injury, and transaminase elevations. The United States Pharmacopeia 2020 comprehensive review confirmed dose-dependent hepatocellular toxicity with adverse events reported from 140 mg to ~1,000 mg EGCG/day. Pre-existing liver disease impairs EGCG clearance and lowers the threshold for injury.
condition_hits.action: Avoid concentrated green tea extract supplements (standardized EGCG ≥400 mg/dose) in patients with liver disease. Brewed green tea is not implicated. Recommend liver function monitoring if use cannot be avoided.
condition_hits.alert_body: High-dose green tea extract has been linked to liver injury. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician before using concentrated EGCG supplements.
condition_hits.informational_note: Concentrated green tea extract has hepatic safety signals relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: EGCG inhibits OATP transporters, reducing beta-blocker absorption and potentially compromising BP control.
drug_class_hits.action: Separate green tea extract from beta-blocker dosing by at least 2-3 hours.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Green tea extract can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding green tea extract.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Green tea extract can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

ingredients.name: Citrus Bioflavonoids Complex
ingredients.notes: A natural blend of flavonoid compounds from citrus fruits — typically including hesperidin, naringin, rutin, and quercetin. These compounds work synergistically with vitamin C, enhancing its absorption and extending its antioxidant activity. Traditionally known as 'Vitamin P.' Supports capillary strength, circulation, and anti-inflammatory pathways.

ingredients.name: Hesperidin Complex
ingredients.notes: The dominant flavanone in oranges and other citrus fruits. Clinically studied for cardiovascular benefits — supports healthy blood pressure, reduces inflammation, and improves endothelial function. Also available as hesperidin methyl chalcone (HMC), which has improved water solubility. Its metabolite hesperetin is the active form.

ingredients.name: Quercetin
ingredients.notes: The standard unenhanced quercetin form — a yellow crystalline powder with notoriously poor absorption (~2% per Hollman 1995). Most of it passes through your gut unabsorbed. Still the most widely sold form because it's cheap. Works better when taken with fat or paired with bromelain/vitamin C, but even then absorption is limited compared to enhanced forms.

condition_hits.mechanism: Quercetin inhibits thyroid peroxidase (TPO), the enzyme responsible for iodinating tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin to form T3 and T4. At high supplemental doses (>500 mg/day), this TPO inhibition may impair thyroid hormone synthesis, contributing to goitre formation or worsening hypothyroidism, particularly in individuals with borderline iodine intake.
condition_hits.action: Use with caution in thyroid disorders, especially hypothyroidism. Limit dose to under 500 mg/day. Monitor TSH periodically and inform endocrinologist of use.
condition_hits.alert_body: Quercetin can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding quercetin and monitor your labs.
condition_hits.informational_note: Quercetin affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Quercetin inhibits CYP2C9 (the primary metabolic enzyme for S-warfarin) and inhibits P-glycoprotein. Both mechanisms reduce warfarin clearance, potentially increasing plasma warfarin concentration and INR. Quercetin also inhibits platelet aggregation independently.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor INR frequently when starting or stopping quercetin supplementation in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber to dose and any INR changes.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Quercetin has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding quercetin and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Quercetin can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

ingredients.notes: A natural blend of flavonoid compounds from citrus fruits — typically including hesperidin, naringin, rutin, and quercetin. These compounds work synergistically with vitamin C, enhancing its absorption and extending its antioxidant activity. Traditionally known as 'Vitamin P.' Supports capillary strength, circulation, and anti-inflammatory pathways.

ingredients.name: Boron
ingredients.notes: The label doesn't specify which boron form is used. All boron forms absorb at >98%, so the conservative score here reflects uncertainty about tolerability and clinical evidence rather than absorption. Check your label for FruiteX-B or glycinate for a more specific score.

ingredients.name: Lutein
ingredients.notes: Xanthophyll carotenoid for macular and eye health, extracted from marigold (Tagetes erecta). Absorption is highly fat-dependent: <5% fasted, 10-50% with dietary fat (PMC5306026, PMC6054194). Free lutein and lutein esters show clinically equivalent bioavailability — conflicting 17% AUC difference across studies (Norkus 2010, PMID:21677121 vs Bowen 2002, PMID:12134711). FloraGLO (free lutein, used in AREDS2 NIH trial) and Lutemax 2020 are well-studied branded forms but not chemically distinct. Must be taken with fat-containing meal for meaningful absorption.

ingredients.name: Lycopene
ingredients.notes: Carotenoid antioxidant standardized from tomato oleoresin. Accelerator mass spectrometry (AMS) study found average ~24% absorption from purified lycopene dose in humans (Ross 2011, PMID:21543537). Absorption is highly fat-dependent (requires 10-15g dietary fat), matrix-dependent (processed > raw tomato), and subject to large inter-individual variability due to genetic polymorphisms in absorption transporters (PMID:25772008). LycoRed/Lyc-O-Mato is the leading standardized brand. Prostate, cardiovascular, and skin health benefits supported by observational and interventional studies.

ingredients.name: Tocotrienol Complex
ingredients.notes: The supplement label does not specify which form of vitamin E is used. This matters greatly — natural d-alpha-tocopherol has ~2x the biological activity of synthetic dl-alpha-tocopherol. Check for 'd-' (natural) vs 'dl-' (synthetic) prefix.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin E is essential, but excessive supplemental intake above established UL is not recommended in pregnancy.
condition_hits.action: Keep total daily vitamin E intake within guideline ranges and avoid megadosing.
condition_hits.alert_headline: Keep vitamin E within prenatal range
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; high intakes can raise adverse-effect risk over time. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

inactive_ingredients.name: Maltodextrin
inactive_ingredients.notes: Highly processed glucose polymer used as filler, binder, and carrier in supplements. GI 85-105 makes it metabolically equivalent to glucose. In vitro data suggests potential gut barrier effects but clinical significance at supplement doses is uncertain. Moderate severity due to high glycemic impact and filler quality signal.
inactive_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Highly processed glucose polymer (DE 3-20) with glycemic index 85-105, comparable to pure glucose. Rapidly digested to glucose in the small intestine. In vitro studies suggest maltodextrin may suppress antimicrobial defense mechanisms and promote E. coli adhesion to intestinal epithelial cells (PMID 22394256). Used as cheap filler/binder in supplements with no nutritional benefit.
population_warnings: Diabetes or insulin resistance — GI 85-105, causes rapid blood glucose spikes comparable to pure glucose
IBD (Crohn's disease) — in vitro evidence of impaired antimicrobial defense and promoted E. coli adhesion (PMID 22394256)
Celiac disease — wheat-derived maltodextrin may contain trace gluten

inactive_ingredients.name: Medium Chain Triglycerides
inactive_ingredients.notes: Easily absorbed fatty acids derived from coconut or palm kernel oil. Used as carrier for fat-soluble vitamins and active ingredients. Generally well-tolerated.

inactive_ingredients.name: natural Vanilla flavor
inactive_ingredients.notes: Flavoring substances derived from plant or animal sources. While generally recognized as safe, the term can encompass hundreds of compounds. Must be derived from natural sources per FDA regulations. Good clean label choice.

inactive_ingredients.name: Sucralose
inactive_ingredients.notes: An artificial sweetener made by chlorinating sugar. 2024 studies show it disrupts the gut microbiome, inhibits bile acid metabolism, and may induce glucose intolerance. Releases harmful compounds when heated. May affect insulin response despite being non-caloric.
inactive_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Chlorinated artificial sweetener that disrupts gut microbiome composition and diversity. Inhibits farnesoid X receptor signaling and disrupts bile acid metabolism. May induce glucose intolerance and affect insulin response. Releases potentially harmful chlorinated compounds when heated above 120°C (248°F).
population_warnings: Gut microbiome sensitivity — multiple studies show sucralose alters gut bacterial composition at approved use levels
Diabetes — may affect glucose/insulin response despite being non-caloric (contested evidence)
Cooking/baking — thermal degradation produces chlorinated compounds (chloropropanols) at >120°C
inactive_ingredients.harmful_notes: Chlorinated artificial sweetener that disrupts gut microbiome composition and diversity. Inhibits farnesoid X receptor signaling and disrupts bile acid metabolism. May induce glucose intolerance and affect insulin response. Releases potentially harmful chlorinated compounds when heated above 120°C (248°F).

warnings.title: High-risk ingredient: Titanium Dioxide
warnings.detail: In 2021, EFSA concluded that a concern for genotoxicity of titanium dioxide (E171) particles 'cannot be ruled out' and that E171 'can no longer be considered safe when used as a food additive,' so no ADI could be established (EFSA Journal 2021;19(5):e06585). On this basis, the European Commission adopted Commission Regulation (EU) 2022/63 banning E171 as a food additive; from August 7 2022 it may no longer be added to foods or food supplements in the EU. FDA still permits TiO2 at ≤1% of food weight (21 CFR 73.575) but initiated reassessment following the EFSA opinion. IARC Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic by inhalation). Nanoparticle form (<100nm) shows greater intestinal absorption and genotoxic concern.

source_urls.title: EFSA 2021: Re-evaluation of titanium dioxide (E171) as a food additive

warnings.safety_warning: A white-pigment additive EFSA ruled in 2021 could no longer be considered safe due to possible genotoxicity. Banned in EU food since 2022; still permitted in US supplements. Avoid when possible.
warnings.safety_warning_one_liner: EU-banned white pigment. Avoid when possible.

warnings.title: Allergen: Milk
warnings.detail: Presence: contains. labelText.parsed.allergens: milk
warnings.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Severity moderate: milk is the 3rd most common cause of pediatric food anaphylaxis (after peanut and tree nuts). Casein and whey are the two primary allergenic protein fractions; casein allergy tends to be more persistent and severe. Lactose intolerance (non-immune) is distinct from milk allergy (IgE-mediated) but both require flagging. Probiotic strains grown on milk media may carry trace proteins.
warnings.supplement_context: Common in protein supplements, some probiotics, and tablets/capsules (lactose).

warnings.title: Vitamin A / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Excess preformed vitamin A intake in pregnancy is associated with teratogenic risk.
warnings.action: Avoid high-dose preformed vitamin A in pregnancy unless clinician-directed.
warnings.alert_headline: Keep preformed vitamin A within prenatal range
warnings.alert_body: Preformed vitamin A (retinol) is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; beta-carotene is not. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total preformed retinol intake from all sources and talk to your clinician.
warnings.informational_note: Preformed retinol is dose-sensitive — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Vitamin A / liver_disease
warnings.detail: Retinol (preformed vitamin A) is stored in hepatic stellate cells (Ito cells) as retinyl esters. Chronic liver disease impairs retinol clearance and stellate cell function, causing accumulation even at doses below standard UL. Hypervitaminosis A induces stellate cell hypertrophy and proliferation, leading to progressive fibrosis, non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, and eventual cirrhosis. CYP2E1 induction (common in alcoholic liver disease) enhances retinol hepatotoxicity via microsomal oxidative activation. Clinically relevant injury has been reported at supplemental doses ≥25,000 IU/day in individuals with underlying liver disease.
warnings.action: Limit supplemental preformed vitamin A (retinol/retinyl esters) to ≤5,000 IU/day in patients with liver disease. Beta-carotene is preferred. Monitor LFTs and clinical signs of hypervitaminosis (hepatomegaly, ascites). Avoid multivitamins with high retinol doses.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin A is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Vitamin A.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin A depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / bleeding_disorders
warnings.detail: Vitamin D does not directly affect platelet aggregation at physiological or supplemental doses. However, high-dose vitamin D supplementation may interact with warfarin sensitivity in patients with concurrent vitamin K deficiency by affecting the vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X). The vitamin D endocrine system and vitamin K share overlapping roles in gamma-carboxylation of hepatic Gla-proteins.
warnings.action: Vitamin D at standard supplemental doses (up to 4000 IU/day) is not a clinically significant bleeding risk on its own. Monitor if taking high-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) with anticoagulants.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding Vitamin D.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / autoimmune
warnings.detail: Vitamin D exerts broad immunomodulatory effects, generally shifting immune responses toward tolerance via Treg cell upregulation and Th17 suppression. Low vitamin D status is associated with increased autoimmune risk. Supplementation may be beneficial in Hashimoto thyroiditis and multiple sclerosis, but high doses in active autoimmune disease require monitoring.
warnings.action: Vitamin D supplementation at standard doses (1000–4000 IU/day) is generally safe in autoimmune conditions. High-dose vitamin D requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / heart_disease
warnings.detail: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, and moderate supplementation is generally considered beneficial. However, pharmacologic doses (>4000 IU/day chronically) can cause hypercalcemia, which promotes vascular calcification and may worsen coronary artery disease. The benefit-risk ratio is dose-dependent.
warnings.action: Target serum 25(OH)D levels of 30–60 ng/mL. Avoid high-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) without clinical monitoring of calcium and 25(OH)D levels.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding Vitamin D.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / ttc
warnings.detail: Vitamin D receptors (VDR) are expressed in ovarian granulosa cells, endometrium, and placenta. A 2017 review (Voulgaris et al., Hormones) summarized strong associations between vitamin D status and PCOS metabolic parameters, endometriosis, and IVF outcomes, with deficiency linked to lower clinical pregnancy rates. Meta-analyses of IVF studies suggest women with 25(OH)D >20 ng/mL have significantly better outcomes, though causality is not fully established.
warnings.action: Check 25(OH)D levels prior to or during TTC. Correct deficiency (<20 ng/mL) with supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) under clinician guidance. Achieving sufficiency (30–60 ng/mL) is recommended before and during early pregnancy. Standard prenatal vitamins provide ~400–1000 IU/day.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin D with your clinician.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / anticoagulants
warnings.detail: High-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) has been associated with altered warfarin sensitivity in some patients. The proposed mechanism involves shared metabolic pathways with vitamin K: vitamin D may modulate hepatic expression of vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X), and vitamin D deficiency itself affects coagulation factor synthesis. Vitamin D and K have documented synergistic effects on Gla-protein carboxylation, and disruption of this balance at supraphysiological vitamin D doses may affect INR stability in warfarin patients.
warnings.action: Monitor INR stability when significantly changing vitamin D supplementation dose, particularly at high doses (>10,000 IU/day). Routine supplementation doses (1000-4000 IU/day) typically do not require INR monitoring changes.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / antiplatelets
warnings.detail: No established direct interaction between vitamin D supplementation and antiplatelet drug activity. Monitoring recommended at high doses due to broader hemostatic pathway involvement.
warnings.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses. Monitor for unusual bleeding if taking high-dose vitamin D with antiplatelet medications.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / nsaids
warnings.detail: No established pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between vitamin D and NSAIDs on platelet or coagulation pathways. Theoretical concern at high doses given shared anti-inflammatory regulatory roles.
warnings.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses.
warnings.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

warnings.title: Vitamin D / immunosuppressants
warnings.detail: Vitamin D has additive immunomodulatory effects that may complement immunosuppressant therapy. Standard-dose supplementation is generally considered safe alongside immunosuppressants and may reduce corticosteroid-induced bone loss.
warnings.action: Standard vitamin D supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels annually. High-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) requires physician oversight.
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

warnings.title: Niacin / diabetes
warnings.detail: At pharmacologic doses (>=1g/day), niacin increases hepatic glucose output and decreases insulin sensitivity. FPG increases of 4-5% and HbA1c increases up to 0.3% documented.
warnings.action: Can worsen glycemic control. Monitor glucose after initiating niacin or increasing dose. Low-dose niacin in multivitamins is not a concern.
warnings.alert_body: Niacin can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding Niacin and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
warnings.informational_note: Niacin affects glycemic control — relevant to people with diabetes.

warnings.title: Niacin / liver_disease
warnings.detail: Niacin at doses exceeding 2 g/day causes dose-dependent hepatotoxicity via impairment of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and calcium homeostasis disruption. Sustained-release formulations are associated with higher hepatotoxicity risk than immediate-release at equivalent doses. Case reports include acute fulminant hepatic failure requiring ICU admission. In patients with pre-existing liver disease, baseline impaired hepatic metabolism reduces the threshold for injury, and niacin-induced vasodilatation may also exacerbate portal hypertension.
warnings.action: Use with caution in liver disease; doses above 500 mg/day should require physician supervision and baseline LFTs. Avoid sustained-release niacin formulations in patients with liver disease. Discontinue if ALT/AST elevations exceed 3x ULN.
warnings.alert_body: Niacin is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Niacin.
warnings.informational_note: Niacin depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

warnings.title: Niacin / high_cholesterol
warnings.detail: Pharmacologic-dose niacin (≥1g/day) raises HDL-C and lowers triglycerides and LDL-C, but large RCTs (AIM-HIGH, HPS2-THRIVE) failed to show cardiovascular benefit when added to statin therapy, and HPS2-THRIVE found excess serious adverse events with niacin. Extended-release niacin combined with laropiprant was withdrawn in Europe. At doses ≥1g/day, niacin also impairs glucose tolerance.
warnings.action: High-dose niacin for cholesterol management has fallen out of favor due to lack of incremental cardiovascular benefit on top of statin therapy and significant side effects. Consult prescriber before initiating pharmacologic niacin for lipid management.
warnings.alert_body: Niacin can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding Niacin — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
warnings.informational_note: Niacin affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

warnings.title: Niacin / hypoglycemics
warnings.detail: Niacin can raise blood glucose, potentially requiring adjustment of diabetes medication doses.
warnings.action: Monitor glucose closely. Diabetes medication dose adjustment may be needed.
warnings.alert_body: Niacin can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding Niacin and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
warnings.informational_note: Niacin affects glycemic control — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

warnings.title: Niacin / statins
warnings.detail: High-dose niacin combined with statins increases the risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis beyond that of statin monotherapy. This risk is particularly elevated with gemfibrozil-statin combinations, but niacin-statin combinations also carry increased myopathy risk, estimated at approximately 0.12% incidence in combination therapy trials. The mechanism involves pharmacodynamic additive muscle toxicity.
warnings.action: If high-dose niacin (≥1g/day) is co-prescribed with statins, monitor for myopathy symptoms (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine). Obtain baseline and periodic CK levels. Avoid high-intensity statin doses in this combination.
warnings.alert_body: Niacin shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding Niacin and watch for new muscle aches.
warnings.informational_note: Niacin affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

warnings.title: Vitamin B6 / lactation
warnings.detail: High-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6) has been investigated as a pharmacologic agent to suppress postpartum lactation. At doses of 450–600 mg/day for 5–7 days, two trials found ~95% inhibition of lactation, though four other studies failed to demonstrate efficacy by prolactin measurement or clinical assessment. The proposed mechanism is dopaminergic: pyridoxine is a cofactor in dopamine synthesis; elevated dopamine tonically inhibits prolactin release from the anterior pituitary. A 1979 NEJM case report warned of dangers of vitamin B6 in nursing mothers at high doses. Normal multivitamin doses (1.9 mg/day recommended for lactation) are not a concern.
warnings.action: Standard prenatal vitamin doses of B6 are safe during lactation. High-dose B6 supplementation (>50 mg/day) during established breastfeeding may suppress milk production. Avoid high-dose B6 if breastfeeding is intended. Consult lactation specialist before use.
warnings.alert_headline: High-dose B6 may affect milk supply
warnings.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
warnings.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Vitamin B6 / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Normal doses of B6 are essential in pregnancy. High pharmacologic doses during lactation may suppress milk production via dopaminergic inhibition of prolactin.
warnings.action: This rule applies to supplemental/pharmacologic doses. Prenatal vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate and encouraged.
warnings.alert_headline: High-dose B6 may affect milk supply
warnings.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
warnings.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

warnings.detail: Normal doses of B6 are essential in pregnancy. High pharmacologic doses during lactation may suppress milk production via dopaminergic inhibition of prolactin.
warnings.action: This rule applies to supplemental/pharmacologic doses. Prenatal vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate and encouraged.
