warnings.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
warnings.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Folic Acid / ttc
warnings.detail: Periconceptional folic acid/folate supplementation reduces neural tube defect (NTD) risk by 69% (RR 0.31, 95% CI 0.17–0.58) in Cochrane meta-analysis of 5 RCTs (6708 births). The protective effect operates during neural tube closure (days 21–28 post-conception), before many women know they are pregnant. Supplementation should therefore begin at least 1 month (ideally 3 months) before conception. For women with MTHFR polymorphisms, methylfolate (5-MTHF) is preferred as it bypasses the enzymatic conversion step.
warnings.action: Begin folate/folic acid supplementation at least 1 month before attempting conception. Standard dose: 400–800 mcg/day for low-risk women; 4 mg/day (4000 mcg) for women with prior NTD-affected pregnancy. Prefer methylfolate (5-MTHF) if MTHFR variant is known. Continue through first trimester.
warnings.alert_body: Folate used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Folate with your clinician.
warnings.informational_note: Folate is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

warnings.title: Folic Acid / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Folate is essential throughout pregnancy and lactation for maternal and fetal/infant cell synthesis. The TTC rule transitions to ongoing pregnancy/lactation supplementation guidance.
warnings.action: Folate supplementation started periconceptionally should continue throughout pregnancy as part of prenatal vitamin regimen.
warnings.alert_body: Folate started before pregnancy should continue through pregnancy and breastfeeding as part of prenatal support. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
warnings.informational_note: Folate is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Folic Acid / lactation
warnings.detail: Folate is essential throughout pregnancy and lactation for maternal and fetal/infant cell synthesis. The TTC rule transitions to ongoing pregnancy/lactation supplementation guidance.
warnings.action: Folate supplementation started periconceptionally should continue throughout pregnancy as part of prenatal vitamin regimen.
warnings.alert_body: Folate started before pregnancy should continue through pregnancy and breastfeeding as part of prenatal support. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
warnings.informational_note: Folate is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Vitamin B12 / ttc
warnings.detail: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and one-carbon metabolism. B12 deficiency elevates homocysteine via impaired methionine synthase activity, and hyperhomocysteinemia is associated with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), implantation failure, and embryotoxicity. Case-control studies document significantly lower serum B12 in women with recurrent miscarriage vs. controls (mean 197 vs 300 pg/mL, p=0.004 in one Syrian cohort; 178 vs 269 pg/mL, p<0.001 in a Polish cohort). B12 is a standard component of preconception care and prenatal vitamins. Optimizing B12 status before conception is associated with improved fertility outcomes and reduced RPL risk via homocysteine normalization.
warnings.action: Ensure adequate vitamin B12 status (serum B12 >400 pg/mL is optimal preconceptionally) when trying to conceive. Standard preconception supplementation: 2.4–6 mcg/day (adequate intake) up to higher doses if deficient. B12 supplementation is recommended as part of a comprehensive preconception vitamin regimen for all women TTC, and is particularly important for vegans, vegetarians, those with gastric conditions, or those on metformin (which reduces B12 absorption).
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin B12 used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin B12 with your clinician.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin B12 is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

warnings.title: Vitamin B12 / pregnancy
warnings.detail: B12 is an essential nutrient throughout pregnancy and lactation. Deficiency is associated with neural tube defects, developmental delay, and recurrent pregnancy loss via hyperhomocysteinemia. Standard prenatal vitamins contain B12; supplementation should continue throughout pregnancy and during breastfeeding.
warnings.action: B12 supplementation during pregnancy is part of standard prenatal care. Vegans and those with malabsorption (pernicious anemia, gastric surgery, metformin use) need higher supplemental doses.
warnings.alert_body: B12 is essential throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding, and supplementation started pre-conception should continue. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
warnings.informational_note: B12 is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Vitamin B12 / lactation
warnings.detail: B12 is an essential nutrient throughout pregnancy and lactation. Deficiency is associated with neural tube defects, developmental delay, and recurrent pregnancy loss via hyperhomocysteinemia. Standard prenatal vitamins contain B12; supplementation should continue throughout pregnancy and during breastfeeding.
warnings.action: B12 supplementation during pregnancy is part of standard prenatal care. Vegans and those with malabsorption (pernicious anemia, gastric surgery, metformin use) need higher supplemental doses.
warnings.alert_body: B12 is essential throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding, and supplementation started pre-conception should continue. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
warnings.informational_note: B12 is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Biotin / thyroid_disorder
warnings.detail: High-dose biotin (>5 mg/day, common in hair/nail supplements) interferes with biotin-streptavidin immunoassay platforms used for TSH, free T4, free T3, and thyroglobulin measurement. Causes false-low TSH and false-high free thyroid hormone results, mimicking hyperthyroidism. Does not alter actual thyroid function.
warnings.action: Stop biotin supplements at least 2 days before thyroid function testing to prevent assay interference. Inform your healthcare provider about biotin use if thyroid tests are planned.
warnings.alert_body: Biotin can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding Biotin and monitor your labs.
warnings.informational_note: Biotin affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

warnings.title: Calcium / heart_disease
warnings.detail: Calcium supplements (not dietary calcium) are associated with a modestly increased risk of myocardial infarction. Bolland et al. WHI reanalysis found RR 1.24 (95% CI 1.07–1.45) for MI with supplemental calcium. Proposed mechanism: acute hypercalcemia following bolus supplementation may promote coronary arterial calcification and thrombosis. Risk may be attenuated by co-administration with vitamin D.
warnings.action: In individuals with existing heart disease, prefer dietary calcium over supplements. If supplementation is necessary, use the lowest effective dose and split doses to blunt calcium spikes. Discuss with cardiologist.
warnings.alert_body: Calcium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding calcium.
warnings.informational_note: Calcium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

warnings.title: Calcium / thyroid_medications
warnings.detail: Calcium forms insoluble chelate complexes with levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing oral absorption by 20–25%. Effect is dose-dependent and clinically significant at standard supplementation doses (600–1000 mg/day).
warnings.action: Separate calcium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours to prevent absorption interference.
warnings.alert_body: Calcium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing calcium away from your dose.
warnings.informational_note: Calcium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

warnings.title: Iron / liver_disease
warnings.detail: The liver is the principal organ of iron storage and regulation. In liver disease, hepatic hepcidin production is impaired, disrupting iron homeostasis and increasing intestinal iron absorption. Iron supplementation in this context risks exacerbating hepatic iron overload, which drives oxidative stress via the Fenton reaction (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ + •OH), lipid peroxidation, and progressive liver fibrosis. This mechanism is most critical in hereditary hemochromatosis and alcoholic liver disease, where iron accumulation is already elevated, but applies broadly to chronic liver disease including NAFLD/NASH, hepatitis C, and cirrhosis.
warnings.action: Avoid routine iron supplementation in liver disease patients unless iron deficiency anemia is confirmed by labs (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC). Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Monitor ferritin and transferrin saturation regularly.
warnings.alert_body: Iron is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of iron.
warnings.informational_note: Iron depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

warnings.title: Iron / antihypertensives
warnings.detail: Iron chelates with methyldopa and certain ACE inhibitors (captopril) in the GI tract, reducing drug absorption and bioavailability.
warnings.action: Separate iron supplement from methyldopa or captopril by at least 2 hours.
warnings.alert_body: Iron can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding iron.
warnings.informational_note: Iron can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

warnings.title: Iron / thyroid_medications
warnings.detail: Ferrous sulfate and other iron salts form stable insoluble complexes with levothyroxine, reducing thyroid hormone absorption. Concurrent ingestion can significantly impair replacement therapy efficacy.
warnings.action: Separate iron supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
warnings.alert_body: Iron can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing iron away from your dose.
warnings.informational_note: Iron affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

warnings.title: Iodine / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Iodine is essential in pregnancy, but excessive intake can adversely affect maternal/fetal thyroid function.
warnings.action: Keep total iodine intake within guideline ranges; avoid excess supplementation.
warnings.alert_body: Iodine is required during pregnancy, but excess can affect maternal and fetal thyroid function. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total iodine intake and talk to your clinician if in doubt.
warnings.informational_note: Iodine is dose-sensitive — both deficiency and excess matter for pregnancy and lactation.

warnings.title: Iodine / thyroid_disorder
warnings.detail: Excess iodine triggers the Wolff-Chaikoff effect, acutely suppressing thyroid hormone synthesis. Individuals with pre-existing thyroid disease (Hashimoto thyroiditis, Graves disease) are particularly susceptible to iodine-induced hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. Kelp/seaweed products contain highly variable iodine loads (50–500% of stated dose) and carry additional risk.
warnings.action: Avoid high-dose iodine or kelp/seaweed supplements in thyroid disorders without physician oversight. Routine supplementation above the RDA (150 mcg/day) is not recommended.
warnings.alert_body: Iodine can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding iodine and monitor your labs.
warnings.informational_note: Iodine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

warnings.title: Iodine / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Both deficiency and excess can be harmful; UL and recommended intake should guide use.
warnings.action: Threshold escalates only on excess; baseline reflects required-but-dose-sensitive nutrient status.
warnings.alert_body: Iodine is required during pregnancy, but excess can affect maternal and fetal thyroid function. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total iodine intake and talk to your clinician if in doubt.
warnings.informational_note: Iodine is dose-sensitive — both deficiency and excess matter for pregnancy and lactation.

warnings.title: Iodine / lactation
warnings.detail: Both deficiency and excess can be harmful; UL and recommended intake should guide use.
warnings.action: Threshold escalates only on excess; baseline reflects required-but-dose-sensitive nutrient status.
warnings.alert_body: Iodine is required during pregnancy, but excess can affect maternal and fetal thyroid function. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total iodine intake and talk to your clinician if in doubt.
warnings.informational_note: Iodine is dose-sensitive — both deficiency and excess matter for pregnancy and lactation.

warnings.title: Iodine / thyroid_medications
warnings.detail: High iodine intake can alter thyroid physiology and therapy response.
warnings.action: Coordinate iodine supplements with thyroid medication management.
warnings.alert_body: Iodine can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing iodine away from your dose.
warnings.informational_note: Iodine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

warnings.title: Magnesium / hypertension
warnings.detail: Magnesium acts as a natural calcium channel blocker, producing modest BP reductions (2-4 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with pharmaceutical calcium channel blockers may enhance hypotensive action.
warnings.action: Generally safe at recommended doses. Inform prescriber if supplementing alongside calcium channel blockers. Monitor for dizziness.
warnings.alert_body: Magnesium can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding magnesium and monitor your readings.
warnings.informational_note: Magnesium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

warnings.title: Magnesium / heart_disease
warnings.detail: Magnesium plays a critical role in cardiac electrophysiology, stabilizing cardiac membranes and modulating ion channels. Hypomagnesemia is associated with cardiac arrhythmias. However, in patients with heart disease who also have renal impairment (common comorbidity), magnesium supplements can accumulate and cause hypermagnesemia, leading to bradycardia, cardiac conduction block, and potentially cardiac arrest.
warnings.action: Use magnesium supplements with caution in heart disease patients, especially those with reduced kidney function. Do not exceed recommended doses without renal function assessment. Monitor for signs of hypermagnesemia (weakness, bradycardia).
warnings.alert_body: Magnesium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding magnesium.
warnings.informational_note: Magnesium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

warnings.title: Magnesium / kidney_disease
warnings.detail: Impaired renal magnesium excretion in CKD (especially eGFR <30) leads to hypermagnesemia accumulation. Supplemental magnesium doses safe in healthy adults can cause life-threatening hypermagnesemia in CKD patients (cardiac arrest, respiratory failure).
warnings.action: Avoid magnesium supplementation in severe CKD (eGFR <30) without nephrologist guidance. In mild-moderate CKD, use with caution and monitor serum Mg levels.
warnings.alert_body: In advanced kidney disease (eGFR below 30), supplemental magnesium can build up to risky levels. If you have CKD, talk to your nephrologist before adding magnesium.
warnings.informational_note: Magnesium excretion depends on kidney function — relevant to people with kidney disease.

warnings.title: Magnesium / antihypertensives
warnings.detail: Additive blood pressure lowering from natural calcium channel blocking activity.
warnings.action: Monitor BP when supplementing alongside antihypertensives.
warnings.alert_body: Magnesium can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding magnesium.
warnings.informational_note: Magnesium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

warnings.title: Magnesium / thyroid_medications
warnings.detail: Divalent magnesium cations can bind levothyroxine and impair gastrointestinal absorption, similar to other divalent mineral-levothyroxine chelation interactions.
warnings.action: Separate magnesium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
warnings.alert_body: Magnesium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing magnesium away from your dose.
warnings.informational_note: Magnesium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

warnings.title: Zinc / thyroid_medications
warnings.detail: Zinc chelation reduces levothyroxine absorption. Zinc is also required for thyroid hormone synthesis; deficiency can cause hypothyroidism, creating a complex bidirectional relationship.
warnings.action: Separate zinc supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
warnings.alert_body: Zinc can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing zinc away from your dose.
warnings.informational_note: Zinc affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

warnings.title: Selenium / thyroid_disorder
warnings.detail: Selenium is essential for iodothyronine deiodinase (type I/II/III) function required to convert T4 to active T3. Selenium supplementation may benefit Hashimoto thyroiditis by reducing thyroid peroxidase antibody titers. However, excess selenium (>400 mcg/day) can paradoxically impair thyroid function and is toxic.
warnings.action: Selenium supplementation in thyroid disorders should be guided by serum selenium levels. Doses above 200 mcg/day should only be used under medical supervision.
warnings.alert_body: Selenium can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding selenium and monitor your labs.
warnings.informational_note: Selenium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

warnings.title: Copper / liver_disease
warnings.detail: Copper homeostasis depends on hepatic biliary excretion as the primary elimination route. In Wilson's disease, ATP7B mutations block copper export into bile, causing pathological hepatic accumulation leading to cirrhosis. In non-Wilson's liver disease, impaired biliary function reduces copper clearance, and supplemental copper can accumulate to hepatotoxic levels even at low doses (>2 mg/day).
warnings.action: Avoid copper supplementation above 2 mg/day in liver disease. In Wilson's disease, supplemental copper is contraindicated. Consult hepatologist before copper supplementation in chronic liver disease.
warnings.alert_body: Copper is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of copper.
warnings.informational_note: Copper depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

warnings.title: Manganese / liver_disease
warnings.detail: Manganese is excreted almost exclusively via bile. Liver disease impairs biliary excretion, leading to systemic accumulation. Excess manganese deposits in the basal ganglia (globus pallidus), producing manganism — a Parkinson-like neurotoxic syndrome with rigidity, bradykinesia, and psychiatric symptoms. MRI T1 hyperintensity in liver disease patients with elevated manganese is well-documented.
warnings.action: Avoid manganese supplementation above 5 mg/day in liver disease. In advanced cirrhosis or liver failure, avoid supplemental manganese entirely. Evaluate manganese status if neurological symptoms develop.
warnings.alert_body: Manganese is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of manganese.
warnings.informational_note: Manganese depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

warnings.title: Chromium / diabetes
warnings.detail: Chromium can alter glucose metabolism and insulin sensitivity.
warnings.action: Use with glucose monitoring if on diabetes treatment.
warnings.alert_body: Chromium can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding chromium and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
warnings.informational_note: Chromium affects glycemic control — relevant to people with diabetes.

warnings.title: Chromium / hypoglycemics
warnings.detail: Potential additive glucose-lowering effect.
warnings.action: Adjust medication only under clinician supervision.
warnings.alert_body: Chromium can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding chromium and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
warnings.informational_note: Chromium affects glycemic control — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

warnings.title: Chromium / thyroid_medications
warnings.detail: Chromium picolinate can reduce levothyroxine absorption when taken concurrently. Divalent chromium binds to thyroid hormone molecules in the gastrointestinal tract.
warnings.action: Separate chromium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
warnings.alert_body: Chromium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing chromium away from your dose.
warnings.informational_note: Chromium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

warnings.title: Alpha-Lipoic Acid / diabetes
warnings.detail: Enhances insulin-stimulated glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocation and reduces oxidative stress-mediated insulin resistance. May potentiate glucose-lowering effects of diabetes medications.
warnings.action: Monitor blood glucose when initiating ALA at doses >= 600 mg/day alongside diabetes medications.
warnings.alert_body: Alpha-lipoic acid can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding Alpha-lipoic acid and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
warnings.informational_note: Alpha-lipoic acid affects glycemic control — relevant to people with diabetes.

warnings.title: Alpha-Lipoic Acid / hypoglycemics
warnings.detail: Additive insulin-sensitizing effect may increase hypoglycemia risk, especially with insulin and sulfonylureas.
warnings.action: Monitor glucose and adjust medication only under clinician supervision.
warnings.alert_body: Alpha-lipoic acid can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding Alpha-lipoic acid and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
warnings.informational_note: Alpha-lipoic acid affects glycemic control — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

warnings.title: Inositol / ttc
warnings.detail: Myo-inositol (the primary isomer in supplements) is a second messenger in insulin signaling and plays a critical role in ovarian follicle maturation. In PCOS, insulin resistance disrupts the inositol phosphoglycan mediator system. Supplementation at 2–4 g/day myo-inositol (alone or in 40:1 ratio with D-chiro-inositol) improves ovulation rates, menstrual regularity, and fertility outcomes. An RCT (Agrawal et al. 2019) found significantly higher live birth rates with metformin + myo-inositol (55%) vs. metformin alone (26.67%, p=0.002) in PCOS women undergoing ovulation induction.
warnings.action: Myo-inositol (2–4 g/day) is a well-supported supplement for women with PCOS who are TTC. It improves ovulation and insulin sensitivity. Safe for TTC use. Also safe to continue in early pregnancy under clinician guidance. Not specifically indicated for non-PCOS TTC without ovulation issues.
warnings.alert_body: Inositol used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of inositol with your clinician.
warnings.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

warnings.title: Inositol / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Myo-inositol is a naturally occurring compound and generally considered safe in pregnancy. Some evidence supports its use to reduce gestational diabetes risk in PCOS pregnancies. Standard monitoring is appropriate.
warnings.action: Transition to clinician-supervised use once pregnancy is confirmed if continuing supplementation.
warnings.alert_body: Myo-inositol is generally considered safe in pregnancy, and use may continue under clinician supervision. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
warnings.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician supervision — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Inositol / lactation
warnings.detail: Myo-inositol is a naturally occurring compound and generally considered safe in pregnancy. Some evidence supports its use to reduce gestational diabetes risk in PCOS pregnancies. Standard monitoring is appropriate.
warnings.action: Transition to clinician-supervised use once pregnancy is confirmed if continuing supplementation.
warnings.alert_body: Myo-inositol is generally considered safe in pregnancy, and use may continue under clinician supervision. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
warnings.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician supervision — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

warnings.title: Green Tea leaf extract / hypertension
warnings.detail: Green tea catechins (EGCG) inhibit intestinal OATP transporters, reducing oral bioavailability of nadolol and potentially other beta-blockers, diminishing BP control.
warnings.action: Avoid taking green tea extract supplements close in time to beta-blockers. Separate doses by several hours.
warnings.alert_body: Green tea extract can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding green tea extract and monitor your readings.
warnings.informational_note: Green tea extract can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

warnings.title: Green Tea leaf extract / liver_disease
warnings.detail: EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), the principal catechin in green tea extract, undergoes hepatic first-pass metabolism. At supplemental doses (≥800 mg EGCG/day), EGCG saturates first-pass elimination and reaches hepatotoxic concentrations, inducing mitochondrial oxidative stress, hepatocellular pattern of liver injury, and transaminase elevations. The United States Pharmacopeia 2020 comprehensive review confirmed dose-dependent hepatocellular toxicity with adverse events reported from 140 mg to ~1,000 mg EGCG/day. Pre-existing liver disease impairs EGCG clearance and lowers the threshold for injury.
warnings.action: Avoid concentrated green tea extract supplements (standardized EGCG ≥400 mg/dose) in patients with liver disease. Brewed green tea is not implicated. Recommend liver function monitoring if use cannot be avoided.
warnings.alert_body: High-dose green tea extract has been linked to liver injury. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician before using concentrated EGCG supplements.
warnings.informational_note: Concentrated green tea extract has hepatic safety signals relevant to people with liver conditions.

warnings.title: Green Tea leaf extract / antihypertensives
warnings.detail: EGCG inhibits OATP transporters, reducing beta-blocker absorption and potentially compromising BP control.
warnings.action: Separate green tea extract from beta-blocker dosing by at least 2-3 hours.
warnings.alert_body: Green tea extract can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding green tea extract.
warnings.informational_note: Green tea extract can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

warnings.title: Quercetin / thyroid_disorder
warnings.detail: Quercetin inhibits thyroid peroxidase (TPO), the enzyme responsible for iodinating tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin to form T3 and T4. At high supplemental doses (>500 mg/day), this TPO inhibition may impair thyroid hormone synthesis, contributing to goitre formation or worsening hypothyroidism, particularly in individuals with borderline iodine intake.
warnings.action: Use with caution in thyroid disorders, especially hypothyroidism. Limit dose to under 500 mg/day. Monitor TSH periodically and inform endocrinologist of use.
warnings.alert_body: Quercetin can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding quercetin and monitor your labs.
warnings.informational_note: Quercetin affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

warnings.title: Quercetin / anticoagulants
warnings.detail: Quercetin inhibits CYP2C9 (the primary metabolic enzyme for S-warfarin) and inhibits P-glycoprotein. Both mechanisms reduce warfarin clearance, potentially increasing plasma warfarin concentration and INR. Quercetin also inhibits platelet aggregation independently.
warnings.action: Monitor INR frequently when starting or stopping quercetin supplementation in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber to dose and any INR changes.
warnings.alert_body: Quercetin has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding quercetin and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
warnings.informational_note: Quercetin can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

warnings.title: Tocotrienol Complex / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Vitamin E is essential, but excessive supplemental intake above established UL is not recommended in pregnancy.
warnings.action: Keep total daily vitamin E intake within guideline ranges and avoid megadosing.
warnings.alert_headline: Keep vitamin E within prenatal range
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; high intakes can raise adverse-effect risk over time. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

warnings.title: Tocotrienol Complex / pregnancy
warnings.detail: Dose-dependent risk profile; high intake can increase adverse-effect risk.
warnings.action: Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.
warnings.alert_headline: Keep vitamin E within prenatal range
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; high intakes can raise adverse-effect risk over time. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
warnings.informational_note: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

warnings.detail: Dose-dependent risk profile; high intake can increase adverse-effect risk.
warnings.action: Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.
warnings.alert_headline: Keep vitamin E within prenatal range
warnings.alert_body: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; high intakes can raise adverse-effect risk over time. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.

warnings.title: Diabetes
warnings.detail: Contains high-glycemic sweeteners: Maltodextrin

warnings_profile_gated.title: High-risk ingredient: Titanium Dioxide
warnings_profile_gated.detail: In 2021, EFSA concluded that a concern for genotoxicity of titanium dioxide (E171) particles 'cannot be ruled out' and that E171 'can no longer be considered safe when used as a food additive,' so no ADI could be established (EFSA Journal 2021;19(5):e06585). On this basis, the European Commission adopted Commission Regulation (EU) 2022/63 banning E171 as a food additive; from August 7 2022 it may no longer be added to foods or food supplements in the EU. FDA still permits TiO2 at ≤1% of food weight (21 CFR 73.575) but initiated reassessment following the EFSA opinion. IARC Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic by inhalation). Nanoparticle form (<100nm) shows greater intestinal absorption and genotoxic concern.

source_urls.title: EFSA 2021: Re-evaluation of titanium dioxide (E171) as a food additive

warnings_profile_gated.safety_warning: A white-pigment additive EFSA ruled in 2021 could no longer be considered safe due to possible genotoxicity. Banned in EU food since 2022; still permitted in US supplements. Avoid when possible.
warnings_profile_gated.safety_warning_one_liner: EU-banned white pigment. Avoid when possible.

warnings_profile_gated.title: Contains Microcrystalline Cellulose
warnings_profile_gated.detail: Inert, indigestible cellulose polymer used as tablet binder and filler. Passes through the GI tract unabsorbed. No evidence of systemic toxicity, nutrient absorption interference, or microbiome disruption at approved use levels. EFSA and JECFA set no ADI limit. Nanoparticle concerns are theoretical and unsubstantiated at food/supplement grades.
warnings_profile_gated.notes: MCC is the most widely used tablet excipient in the supplement and pharmaceutical industry. FDA GRAS with decades of safe use. EFSA and JECFA set no ADI limit. Low severity — tracked purely as non-nutritive filler quality signal.
warnings_profile_gated.mechanism_of_harm: Inert, indigestible cellulose polymer used as tablet binder and filler. Passes through the GI tract unabsorbed. No evidence of systemic toxicity, nutrient absorption interference, or microbiome disruption at approved use levels. EFSA and JECFA set no ADI limit. Nanoparticle concerns are theoretical and unsubstantiated at food/supplement grades.
population_warnings: No specific population concerns at standard excipient levels
warnings_profile_gated.safety_summary: An inert cellulose filler that passes through unabsorbed. Generally considered safe — low overall concern.
warnings_profile_gated.safety_summary_one_liner: Inert cellulose filler — low overall concern.

warnings_profile_gated.title: Contains Maltodextrin
warnings_profile_gated.detail: Highly processed glucose polymer (DE 3-20) with glycemic index 85-105, comparable to pure glucose. Rapidly digested to glucose in the small intestine. In vitro studies suggest maltodextrin may suppress antimicrobial defense mechanisms and promote E. coli adhesion to intestinal epithelial cells (PMID 22394256). Used as cheap filler/binder in supplements with no nutritional benefit.
warnings_profile_gated.notes: Highly processed glucose polymer used as filler, binder, and carrier in supplements. GI 85-105 makes it metabolically equivalent to glucose. In vitro data suggests potential gut barrier effects but clinical significance at supplement doses is uncertain. Moderate severity due to high glycemic impact and filler quality signal.
warnings_profile_gated.mechanism_of_harm: Highly processed glucose polymer (DE 3-20) with glycemic index 85-105, comparable to pure glucose. Rapidly digested to glucose in the small intestine. In vitro studies suggest maltodextrin may suppress antimicrobial defense mechanisms and promote E. coli adhesion to intestinal epithelial cells (PMID 22394256). Used as cheap filler/binder in supplements with no nutritional benefit.
population_warnings: Diabetes or insulin resistance — GI 85-105, causes rapid blood glucose spikes comparable to pure glucose
IBD (Crohn's disease) — in vitro evidence of impaired antimicrobial defense and promoted E. coli adhesion (PMID 22394256)
Celiac disease — wheat-derived maltodextrin may contain trace gluten
warnings_profile_gated.safety_summary: A highly processed glucose polymer with a glycemic index comparable to pure glucose — worth moderating daily intake.

warnings_profile_gated.title: Contains Sucralose
warnings_profile_gated.detail: Chlorinated artificial sweetener that disrupts gut microbiome composition and diversity. Inhibits farnesoid X receptor signaling and disrupts bile acid metabolism. May induce glucose intolerance and affect insulin response. Releases potentially harmful chlorinated compounds when heated above 120°C (248°F).
warnings_profile_gated.notes: An artificial sweetener made by chlorinating sugar. 2024 studies show it disrupts the gut microbiome, inhibits bile acid metabolism, and may induce glucose intolerance. Releases harmful compounds when heated. May affect insulin response despite being non-caloric.
warnings_profile_gated.mechanism_of_harm: Chlorinated artificial sweetener that disrupts gut microbiome composition and diversity. Inhibits farnesoid X receptor signaling and disrupts bile acid metabolism. May induce glucose intolerance and affect insulin response. Releases potentially harmful chlorinated compounds when heated above 120°C (248°F).
population_warnings: Gut microbiome sensitivity — multiple studies show sucralose alters gut bacterial composition at approved use levels
Diabetes — may affect glucose/insulin response despite being non-caloric (contested evidence)
Cooking/baking — thermal degradation produces chlorinated compounds (chloropropanols) at >120°C
warnings_profile_gated.safety_summary: A chlorinated artificial sweetener. Studies link it to changes in gut microbiome and bile-acid signaling — worth noting if you are avoiding artificial sweeteners.
warnings_profile_gated.safety_summary_one_liner: Artificial sweetener linked to gut-microbiome changes.

warnings_profile_gated.title: Allergen: Milk
warnings_profile_gated.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Severity moderate: milk is the 3rd most common cause of pediatric food anaphylaxis (after peanut and tree nuts). Casein and whey are the two primary allergenic protein fractions; casein allergy tends to be more persistent and severe. Lactose intolerance (non-immune) is distinct from milk allergy (IgE-mediated) but both require flagging. Probiotic strains grown on milk media may carry trace proteins.
warnings_profile_gated.supplement_context: Common in protein supplements, some probiotics, and tablets/capsules (lactose).

warnings_profile_gated.title: Vitamin B6 / lactation
warnings_profile_gated.detail: High-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6) has been investigated as a pharmacologic agent to suppress postpartum lactation. At doses of 450–600 mg/day for 5–7 days, two trials found ~95% inhibition of lactation, though four other studies failed to demonstrate efficacy by prolactin measurement or clinical assessment. The proposed mechanism is dopaminergic: pyridoxine is a cofactor in dopamine synthesis; elevated dopamine tonically inhibits prolactin release from the anterior pituitary. A 1979 NEJM case report warned of dangers of vitamin B6 in nursing mothers at high doses. Normal multivitamin doses (1.9 mg/day recommended for lactation) are not a concern.
warnings_profile_gated.action: Standard prenatal vitamin doses of B6 are safe during lactation. High-dose B6 supplementation (>50 mg/day) during established breastfeeding may suppress milk production. Avoid high-dose B6 if breastfeeding is intended. Consult lactation specialist before use.
warnings_profile_gated.alert_headline: High-dose B6 may affect milk supply
warnings_profile_gated.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
warnings_profile_gated.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

warnings_profile_gated.title: Magnesium / kidney_disease
warnings_profile_gated.detail: Impaired renal magnesium excretion in CKD (especially eGFR <30) leads to hypermagnesemia accumulation. Supplemental magnesium doses safe in healthy adults can cause life-threatening hypermagnesemia in CKD patients (cardiac arrest, respiratory failure).
warnings_profile_gated.action: Avoid magnesium supplementation in severe CKD (eGFR <30) without nephrologist guidance. In mild-moderate CKD, use with caution and monitor serum Mg levels.
warnings_profile_gated.alert_body: In advanced kidney disease (eGFR below 30), supplemental magnesium can build up to risky levels. If you have CKD, talk to your nephrologist before adding magnesium.
warnings_profile_gated.informational_note: Magnesium excretion depends on kidney function — relevant to people with kidney disease.

warnings_profile_gated.title: Green Tea leaf extract / liver_disease
warnings_profile_gated.detail: EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), the principal catechin in green tea extract, undergoes hepatic first-pass metabolism. At supplemental doses (≥800 mg EGCG/day), EGCG saturates first-pass elimination and reaches hepatotoxic concentrations, inducing mitochondrial oxidative stress, hepatocellular pattern of liver injury, and transaminase elevations. The United States Pharmacopeia 2020 comprehensive review confirmed dose-dependent hepatocellular toxicity with adverse events reported from 140 mg to ~1,000 mg EGCG/day. Pre-existing liver disease impairs EGCG clearance and lowers the threshold for injury.
warnings_profile_gated.action: Avoid concentrated green tea extract supplements (standardized EGCG ≥400 mg/dose) in patients with liver disease. Brewed green tea is not implicated. Recommend liver function monitoring if use cannot be avoided.
warnings_profile_gated.alert_body: High-dose green tea extract has been linked to liver injury. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician before using concentrated EGCG supplements.
warnings_profile_gated.informational_note: Concentrated green tea extract has hepatic safety signals relevant to people with liver conditions.

synergy_detail.bonus_explanation: This product contains ingredients with proven synergistic effects backed by clinical trials.

clusters.name: Magnesium Nervous System
clusters.mechanism: Vitamin B6 (as P5P) is required for magnesium transport into cells. Taurine stabilizes neuronal membrane potential alongside magnesium. Glycine acts as an inhibitory neurotransmitter, complementing magnesium's NMDA receptor modulation.
clusters.benefit_short: Magnesium with vitamin B6, taurine, or glycine supports calmer nerves and better sleep — these partners help magnesium actually reach your cells.

clusters.name: Sleep Stack
clusters.mechanism: Melatonin regulates circadian signaling; magnesium activates GABA receptors and reduces cortisol; L-theanine promotes alpha-wave relaxation; glycine lowers core body temperature to initiate sleep onset.
clusters.benefit_short: Melatonin, magnesium, L-theanine, and glycine work on different parts of falling asleep — the stack covers wind-down, onset, and staying asleep.

clusters.name: Eye Health
clusters.mechanism: Lutein and zeaxanthin accumulate in the macular pigment, filtering blue light and quenching reactive oxygen species. Zinc supports retinal metabolism. Vitamin C and E provide systemic antioxidant defense for ocular tissues (AREDS2 formulation basis).
clusters.benefit_short: Lutein, zeaxanthin, astaxanthin, and bilberry together filter blue light and protect the retina — they concentrate in different parts of the eye.

clusters.name: Methylation Support
clusters.mechanism: Methylcobalamin and methylfolate serve as methyl donors in the methionine cycle. TMG provides an alternative remethylation pathway via BHMT. Choline feeds into phosphatidylcholine synthesis. B6 (P5P) is required for the transsulfuration pathway converting homocysteine to cysteine.
clusters.benefit_short: Methylated B12 and methylfolate paired with B6 and riboflavin support methylation — helpful for people with MTHFR variants or B-vitamin gaps.

clusters.name: Immune Defense
clusters.mechanism: Vitamin C supports neutrophil function and acts as a cofactor for immune signaling enzymes. Zinc is required for T-cell maturation and NK cell activity. Vitamin D modulates innate immune responses through antimicrobial peptide (cathelicidin) expression. Quercetin has demonstrated ionophore activity
clusters.benefit_short: Vitamin C, zinc, vitamin D, and elderberry work on different immune pathways — giving your defense system multiple angles at once.

clusters.name: Energy + Mitochondria
clusters.mechanism: CoQ10 is an essential electron carrier in the mitochondrial respiratory chain (Complex I to III). Acetyl-L-carnitine shuttles long-chain fatty acids across the inner mitochondrial membrane for beta-oxidation. PQQ stimulates mitochondrial biogenesis. Creatine buffers ATP through the phosphocreatine s
clusters.benefit_short: Acetyl-L-carnitine, CoQ10, PQQ, and B vitamins fuel your cells' energy factories (mitochondria) — helpful when you feel low-energy.

clusters.name: Stress Resilience
clusters.mechanism: Ashwagandha modulates the HPA axis by attenuating cortisol release. Rhodiola activates AMPK and modulates monoamine neurotransmitters. L-theanine promotes alpha-wave brain activity without sedation. Magnesium is a cofactor for over 300 enzymatic reactions including those in the stress response.
clusters.benefit_short: Ashwagandha, rhodiola, L-theanine, and phosphatidylserine support calmer stress response — adaptogens and calming nutrients combined.

clusters.name: Cognitive Performance
clusters.mechanism: Bacopa upregulates tryptophan hydroxylase and serotonin transporter expression, improving synaptic communication. Alpha-GPC provides choline for acetylcholine synthesis. Lion's mane stimulates nerve growth factor (NGF) production. DHA maintains neuronal membrane fluidity critical for synaptic transm
clusters.benefit_short: Bacopa, alpha-GPC, citicoline, and lion's mane support focus and learning — different angles on brain chemistry and nerve growth.

clusters.name: Cardiovascular Support
clusters.mechanism: CoQ10 supports mitochondrial energy production in cardiomyocytes and acts as a lipid-soluble antioxidant. Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) reduces triglycerides and dampens vascular inflammation. Vitamin K2 (MK-7) activates matrix Gla-protein which inhibits arterial calcification. Magnesium supports vascular smoot
clusters.benefit_short: CoQ10, omega-3, magnesium, and hawthorn together support heart rhythm, blood-vessel health, and cholesterol — a well-rounded heart stack.

clusters.name: Blood Sugar Regulation
clusters.mechanism: Berberine activates AMPK, increasing glucose uptake independent of insulin. Chromium enhances insulin receptor signaling. Alpha-lipoic acid improves insulin sensitivity and glucose disposal. Cinnamon contains polyphenols that mimic insulin activity at the receptor level.
clusters.benefit_short: Berberine, chromium, alpha-lipoic acid, and cinnamon work on different parts of glucose handling — helpful for blood-sugar balance.

clusters.name: Hormone Balance (Men)
clusters.mechanism: Zinc is essential for testosterone synthesis and 5-alpha-reductase regulation. Ashwagandha supports testosterone via cortisol reduction (inverse relationship). Vitamin D acts as a steroid hormone precursor. Tongkat ali may support free testosterone by reducing SHBG binding.
clusters.benefit_short: Zinc, ashwagandha, vitamin D, and tongkat ali support healthy testosterone pathways — a common stack for men's hormonal balance.

clusters.name: Hormone Balance (Women)
clusters.mechanism: Vitex (chasteberry) modulates prolactin through dopaminergic activity. DIM promotes favorable 2-hydroxyestrone metabolism over 16-alpha-hydroxyestrone. Black cohosh acts on serotonergic receptors relevant to thermoregulation. Maca supports hormonal balance without directly altering circulating hormo
clusters.benefit_short: Chasteberry, DIM, calcium d-glucarate, and B6 support estrogen balance and menstrual comfort — a common stack for cycle support.

clusters.name: Hair & Skin Nutrition
clusters.mechanism: Biotin is a cofactor for carboxylase enzymes critical in keratin synthesis. Collagen peptides provide hydroxyproline and glycine as structural protein precursors. Vitamin C is required for procollagen hydroxylation. Zinc supports keratinocyte differentiation.
clusters.benefit_short: Biotin, silica, zinc, collagen, and vitamin C support hair, skin, and nail building blocks — beauty nutrition in layers.

clusters.name: Bone Health
clusters.mechanism: Calcium provides building blocks; Vitamin D enhances absorption; K2 (MK-7) activates osteocalcin to deposit calcium in bones; Magnesium is cofactor for vitamin D activation.
clusters.benefit_short: Calcium, vitamin D, K2, magnesium, and boron work together for bone strength — calcium alone isn't enough, these partners guide it to bone.

clusters.name: Iron Absorption
clusters.mechanism: Vitamin C reduces ferric iron (Fe3+) to ferrous form (Fe2+) at the brush border, dramatically increasing non-heme iron absorption. Folate and B12 are co-factors in erythropoiesis. Lactoferrin facilitates iron transport across the intestinal epithelium.
clusters.benefit_short: Iron with vitamin C and folate is absorbed better and supports red-blood-cell production — the partners help iron do its job.

clusters.name: Antioxidant Defense
clusters.mechanism: Vitamin C (aqueous phase) and vitamin E (lipid phase) form a redox couple — C regenerates oxidized E. Selenium is required for glutathione peroxidase catalytic activity. CoQ10 prevents lipid peroxidation initiation in mitochondrial membranes. NAC replenishes the glutathione pool.
clusters.benefit_short: Vitamin C, vitamin E, selenium, and CoQ10 regenerate each other — the network is stronger than any single antioxidant alone.

clusters.name: Thyroid Support
clusters.mechanism: Iodine is the essential substrate for T3 and T4 thyroid hormone synthesis. Selenium is required for deiodinase enzymes that convert T4 to active T3. Zinc supports thyroid hormone receptor binding. Tyrosine is the amino acid backbone of thyroid hormones.
clusters.benefit_short: Iodine, L-tyrosine, selenium, and zinc together supply thyroid hormone building blocks and the nutrients needed to convert to active form.

clusters.name: Fat Metabolism
clusters.mechanism: L-carnitine shuttles long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria for beta-oxidation. EGCG (green tea) inhibits COMT, prolonging norepinephrine-mediated thermogenesis. CLA modulates PPAR-gamma activity in adipocytes. Chromium enhances insulin-mediated glucose disposal, supporting metabolic flexibility.
clusters.benefit_short: L-carnitine, green tea extract, CLA, and forskolin support how the body uses fat for energy — modest but complementary effects.

clusters.name: Inflammation Control
clusters.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits NF-kB transcription factor, reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine expression. Boswellia (AKBA) selectively inhibits 5-lipoxygenase. Omega-3 (EPA) serves as a precursor for anti-inflammatory resolvins and protectins. Quercetin stabilizes mast cells and inhibits lipoxygenase.
clusters.benefit_short: Omega-3, curcumin, boswellia, and quercetin calm different inflammatory pathways — together they cover more than any one alone.

clusters.name: Adrenal Support
clusters.mechanism: Ashwagandha modulates HPA axis signaling and attenuates cortisol hypersecretion. Rhodiola activates stress-protective HSP70 heat shock proteins. Pantothenic acid (B5) is a precursor to coenzyme A, essential for adrenal steroidogenesis. Magnesium is consumed during stress responses and requires reple
clusters.benefit_short: Vitamin C, B5, ashwagandha, and rhodiola support adrenal function during stress — nutrients plus adaptogens for tougher stretches.

clusters.name: Electrolyte Balance
clusters.mechanism: Sodium, potassium, and magnesium maintain cellular membrane potential and osmotic balance. Potassium citrate supports intracellular fluid volume. Magnesium is required for Na+/K+-ATPase pump function. Balanced electrolytes prevent exercise-induced cramping and dehydration.
clusters.benefit_short: Sodium, potassium, magnesium, and calcium in balance support hydration, nerve function, and muscle contraction — especially during exercise.

clusters.name: Skin Health Complex
clusters.mechanism: Collagen peptides provide bioactive hydroxyproline-containing dipeptides that stimulate fibroblast activity. Hyaluronic acid supports dermal hydration. Vitamin C is required for prolyl hydroxylase in collagen cross-linking. Astaxanthin protects skin from UV-induced oxidative damage.
clusters.benefit_short: Hyaluronic acid, ceramides, collagen, vitamin C, and zinc together support skin hydration, barrier, and collagen production — inside-out beauty.

clusters.name: Omega-3 Absorption Enhancement
clusters.mechanism: Phospholipid-bound omega-3s have 10x better absorption than ethyl esters. Astaxanthin protects omega-3s from oxidation. Take with fat for optimal absorption.
clusters.benefit_short: Omega-3 as triglyceride or phospholipid form, taken with food, is absorbed much better than ethyl ester forms on an empty stomach.

clusters.name: Collagen Synthesis Support
clusters.mechanism: Vitamin C is essential cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase (collagen synthesis enzymes). Copper enables cross-linking via lysyl oxidase. Lysine/proline are primary collagen amino acids.
clusters.benefit_short: Vitamin C, lysine, proline, and copper together give your body the building blocks and cofactors needed to make collagen — the core of skin and joints.

clusters.name: Respiratory Health & Lung Support
clusters.mechanism: NAC is mucolytic and boosts glutathione. Quercetin has antiviral properties enhanced by vitamin C (which recycles quercetin). Zinc supports immune function. Bromelain reduces inflammation.
clusters.benefit_short: NAC, quercetin, vitamin C, and vitamin D together support healthy lung function — antioxidant and immune angles for breathing comfort.

clusters.name: Prostate Health
clusters.mechanism: Saw palmetto inhibits 5-alpha reductase. Beta-sitosterol supports urinary flow. Zinc is highly concentrated in prostate tissue. Lycopene is a prostate-specific antioxidant.
clusters.benefit_short: Saw palmetto, beta-sitosterol, pygeum, and zinc together support healthy prostate function — a commonly studied men's-health stack.

clusters.name: Focus & Attention Support
clusters.mechanism: DHA is critical for brain cell membranes. Phosphatidylserine supports neurotransmitter function. Zinc and iron deficiencies are linked to attention issues. L-theanine + caffeine enhance focus synergistically.
clusters.benefit_short: Omega-3 (DHA/EPA), phosphatidylserine, and L-tyrosine together support focus and attention — structural plus neurotransmitter support.

clusters.name: Menopause & Perimenopause Support
clusters.mechanism: Black cohosh and red clover provide phytoestrogens for hot flash relief. Evening primrose supports hormonal balance. Sage specifically targets hot flashes and night sweats. Maca supports overall hormonal adaptation.
clusters.benefit_short: Black cohosh, red clover, evening primrose, and sage together address hot flashes, hormonal balance, and night sweats — layered symptom support.

clusters.name: Prenatal & Pregnancy Support
clusters.mechanism: Folate prevents neural tube defects. Iron supports increased blood volume. DHA is critical for fetal brain/eye development. Choline supports neural development. Iodine supports thyroid and fetal brain development.
clusters.benefit_short: Methylfolate, iron, choline, DHA, and iodine together cover the nutrients most needed during pregnancy — aligned with prenatal guidelines.

clusters.name: Nerve Health & Neuropathy Support
clusters.mechanism: B vitamins (especially B1, B6, B12) are essential for nerve function. Alpha-lipoic acid is proven for diabetic neuropathy. Acetyl-L-carnitine supports nerve regeneration. Benfotiamine has superior bioavailability for nerve tissue.
clusters.benefit_short: B-complex with benfotiamine, B6, B12, alpha-lipoic acid, and acetyl-L-carnitine together support nerve health — a common neuropathy stack.

clusters.name: Histamine & Allergy Support
clusters.mechanism: Quercetin stabilizes mast cells and inhibits histamine release. Vitamin C is a natural antihistamine and enhances quercetin. Bromelain improves quercetin absorption. DAO enzyme breaks down dietary histamine.
clusters.benefit_short: Quercetin, vitamin C, bromelain, and nettle together support a calmer histamine response — a natural approach to seasonal allergies.

clusters.name: Kidney Health Support
clusters.mechanism: Astragalus and cordyceps have nephroprotective properties. Omega-3s reduce inflammation. CoQ10 supports mitochondrial function in kidney cells. Alpha-lipoic acid provides antioxidant protection.
clusters.benefit_short: Astragalus, cordyceps, alpha-lipoic acid, and omega-3 together support kidney function — traditional herbs plus modern antioxidants.

clusters.name: Dental & Oral Health
clusters.mechanism: CoQ10 supports mitochondrial energy in gingival fibroblasts and has demonstrated benefit in periodontal inflammation. Vitamin D enhances calcium absorption for enamel remineralization and modulates oral immune defenses. Vitamin C is critical for collagen synthesis in gingival connective tissue. Zinc
clusters.benefit_short: CoQ10, vitamin D, K2, and probiotics together support gum and tooth health from the inside — beyond just brushing and flossing.

clusters.name: Wound Healing
clusters.mechanism: Zinc is essential for cell division and immune function at wound sites. Vitamin C is required for collagen hydroxylation and cross-linking in new connective tissue. Collagen peptides provide structural amino acid precursors (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline). Vitamin A regulates epithelial cell grow
clusters.benefit_short: Zinc, vitamin C, collagen, arginine, and glutamine together support tissue repair — building blocks plus immune and circulation support.

clusters.name: Migraine Prevention
clusters.mechanism: Magnesium blocks cortical spreading depression (the neurophysiological substrate of migraine aura) and modulates NMDA receptors. Riboflavin (B2) at high dose improves mitochondrial energy metabolism in neurons with impaired oxidative phosphorylation. CoQ10 supports mitochondrial electron transport i
clusters.benefit_short: Magnesium, riboflavin (B2), CoQ10, and feverfew are the most-studied supplement combo for reducing migraine frequency — the headache stack.

clusters.name: Blood Pressure Support
clusters.mechanism: Magnesium promotes vascular smooth muscle relaxation and acts as a natural calcium channel blocker. CoQ10 improves endothelial function and reduces peripheral vascular resistance. Garlic (allicin) stimulates hydrogen sulfide production, a potent vasodilator. Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) improves arterial compl
clusters.benefit_short: Magnesium, CoQ10, hibiscus, and beet root together support healthy blood-pressure pathways — modest effects that add up in combination.

clusters.name: Fertility (Female)
clusters.mechanism: Folate (as 5-MTHF) is critical for DNA methylation during oocyte maturation and early embryogenesis. CoQ10 supports mitochondrial energy in oocytes, which declines with age and correlates with egg quality. Myo-inositol acts as a second messenger in insulin signaling and improves oocyte quality in PC
clusters.benefit_short: Methylfolate, CoQ10, inositol, vitamin D, and omega-3 together support egg quality and cycle regularity — a common fertility-preparation stack.

clusters.name: Omega-3 + Niacin Lipid Support
clusters.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids lower triglycerides primarily via reduced hepatic VLDL production, while niacin raises HDL via inhibition of hepatic lipase and reduction of HDL catabolism. The combination addresses both legs of dyslipidemia more effectively than either alone. Human trial evidence from PMC106004
clusters.benefit_short: Omega-3 with niacin covers both triglycerides and cholesterol support — a classic combo for overall lipid-panel improvement.

pregnancy.label: Pregnancy
actions: Applies to preformed vitamin A forms; carotenoids are not handled by this threshold rule.
Avoid high-dose preformed vitamin A in pregnancy unless clinician-directed.
B12 supplementation during pregnancy is part of standard prenatal care. Vegans and those with malabsorption (pernicious anemia, gastric surgery, metformin use) need higher supplemental doses.
Folate supplementation started periconceptionally should continue throughout pregnancy as part of prenatal vitamin regimen.
Keep total daily vitamin E intake within guideline ranges and avoid megadosing.
Keep total iodine intake within guideline ranges; avoid excess supplementation.
Keep vitamin C intake within guideline ranges; avoid high-dose supplementation without clinical need.
This rule applies to supplemental/pharmacologic doses. Prenatal vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate and encouraged.
Threshold escalates only on excess; baseline reflects required-but-dose-sensitive nutrient status.
Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.
Transition to clinician-supervised use once pregnancy is confirmed if continuing supplementation.

liver_disease.label: Liver Disease
actions: Avoid concentrated green tea extract supplements (standardized EGCG ≥400 mg/dose) in patients with liver disease. Brewed green tea is not implicated. Recommend liver function monitoring if use cannot be avoided.
Avoid copper supplementation above 2 mg/day in liver disease. In Wilson's disease, supplemental copper is contraindicated. Consult hepatologist before copper supplementation in chronic liver disease.

Avoid manganese supplementation above 5 mg/day in liver disease. In advanced cirrhosis or liver failure, avoid supplemental manganese entirely. Evaluate manganese status if neurological symptoms develop.

Avoid routine iron supplementation in liver disease patients unless iron deficiency anemia is confirmed by labs (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC). Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Monitor ferritin and transferrin saturation regularly.

Limit supplemental preformed vitamin A (retinol/retinyl esters) to ≤5,000 IU/day in patients with liver disease. Beta-carotene is preferred. Monitor LFTs and clinical signs of hypervitaminosis (hepatomegaly, ascites). Avoid multivitamins with high retinol doses.

Use with caution in liver disease; doses above 500 mg/day should require physician supervision and baseline LFTs. Avoid sustained-release niacin formulations in patients with liver disease. Discontinue if ALT/AST elevations exceed 3x ULN.

lactation.label: Breastfeeding
actions: Applies to preformed vitamin A forms; carotenoids are not handled by this threshold rule.
B12 supplementation during pregnancy is part of standard prenatal care. Vegans and those with malabsorption (pernicious anemia, gastric surgery, metformin use) need higher supplemental doses.
Folate supplementation started periconceptionally should continue throughout pregnancy as part of prenatal vitamin regimen.

Standard prenatal vitamin doses of B6 are safe during lactation. High-dose B6 supplementation (>50 mg/day) during established breastfeeding may suppress milk production. Avoid high-dose B6 if breastfeeding is intended. Consult lactation specialist before use.
This rule applies to supplemental/pharmacologic doses. Prenatal vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate and encouraged.
Threshold escalates only on excess; baseline reflects required-but-dose-sensitive nutrient status.
Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.
Transition to clinician-supervised use once pregnancy is confirmed if continuing supplementation.

bleeding_disorders.label: Bleeding Disorders
actions: Vitamin D at standard supplemental doses (up to 4000 IU/day) is not a clinically significant bleeding risk on its own. Monitor if taking high-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) with anticoagulants.

autoimmune.label: Autoimmune Condition
actions: Vitamin D supplementation at standard doses (1000–4000 IU/day) is generally safe in autoimmune conditions. High-dose vitamin D requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity.

heart_disease.label: Heart Disease
actions: In individuals with existing heart disease, prefer dietary calcium over supplements. If supplementation is necessary, use the lowest effective dose and split doses to blunt calcium spikes. Discuss with cardiologist.
Target serum 25(OH)D levels of 30–60 ng/mL. Avoid high-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) without clinical monitoring of calcium and 25(OH)D levels.

Use magnesium supplements with caution in heart disease patients, especially those with reduced kidney function. Do not exceed recommended doses without renal function assessment. Monitor for signs of hypermagnesemia (weakness, bradycardia).

ttc.label: Trying to Conceive
actions: Begin folate/folic acid supplementation at least 1 month before attempting conception. Standard dose: 400–800 mcg/day for low-risk women; 4 mg/day (4000 mcg) for women with prior NTD-affected pregnancy. Prefer methylfolate (5-MTHF) if MTHFR variant is known. Continue through first trimester.

Check 25(OH)D levels prior to or during TTC. Correct deficiency (<20 ng/mL) with supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) under clinician guidance. Achieving sufficiency (30–60 ng/mL) is recommended before and during early pregnancy. Standard prenatal vitamins provide ~400–1000 IU/day.

Ensure adequate vitamin B12 status (serum B12 >400 pg/mL is optimal preconceptionally) when trying to conceive. Standard preconception supplementation: 2.4–6 mcg/day (adequate intake) up to higher doses if deficient. B12 supplementation is recommended as part of a comprehensive preconception vitamin regimen for all women TTC, and is particularly important for vegans, vegetarians, those with gastric conditions, or those on metformin (which reduces B12 absorption).

Myo-inositol (2–4 g/day) is a well-supported supplement for women with PCOS who are TTC. It improves ovulation and insulin sensitivity. Safe for TTC use. Also safe to continue in early pregnancy under clinician guidance. Not specifically indicated for non-PCOS TTC without ovulation issues.

diabetes.label: Diabetes
actions: Can worsen glycemic control. Monitor glucose after initiating niacin or increasing dose. Low-dose niacin in multivitamins is not a concern.
Monitor blood glucose when initiating ALA at doses >= 600 mg/day alongside diabetes medications.
Use with glucose monitoring if on diabetes treatment.

high_cholesterol.label: High Cholesterol
actions: High-dose niacin for cholesterol management has fallen out of favor due to lack of incremental cardiovascular benefit on top of statin therapy and significant side effects. Consult prescriber before initiating pharmacologic niacin for lipid management.

thyroid_disorder.label: Thyroid Condition
actions: Avoid high-dose iodine or kelp/seaweed supplements in thyroid disorders without physician oversight. Routine supplementation above the RDA (150 mcg/day) is not recommended.
Selenium supplementation in thyroid disorders should be guided by serum selenium levels. Doses above 200 mcg/day should only be used under medical supervision.
Stop biotin supplements at least 2 days before thyroid function testing to prevent assay interference. Inform your healthcare provider about biotin use if thyroid tests are planned.
Use with caution in thyroid disorders, especially hypothyroidism. Limit dose to under 500 mg/day. Monitor TSH periodically and inform endocrinologist of use.

hypertension.label: High Blood Pressure
actions: Avoid taking green tea extract supplements close in time to beta-blockers. Separate doses by several hours.
Generally safe at recommended doses. Inform prescriber if supplementing alongside calcium channel blockers. Monitor for dizziness.

kidney_disease.label: Kidney Disease
actions: Avoid magnesium supplementation in severe CKD (eGFR <30) without nephrologist guidance. In mild-moderate CKD, use with caution and monitor serum Mg levels.

anticoagulants.label: Anticoagulants
actions: Monitor INR frequently when starting or stopping quercetin supplementation in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber to dose and any INR changes.

Monitor INR stability when significantly changing vitamin D supplementation dose, particularly at high doses (>10,000 IU/day). Routine supplementation doses (1000-4000 IU/day) typically do not require INR monitoring changes.

antiplatelets.label: Antiplatelet Agents
actions: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses. Monitor for unusual bleeding if taking high-dose vitamin D with antiplatelet medications.

nsaids.label: NSAIDs
actions: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses.

immunosuppressants.label: Immunosuppressants
actions: Standard vitamin D supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels annually. High-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) requires physician oversight.

hypoglycemics.label: Glucose-Lowering Medications
actions: Adjust medication only under clinician supervision.
Monitor glucose and adjust medication only under clinician supervision.
Monitor glucose closely. Diabetes medication dose adjustment may be needed.

statins.label: Statins
actions: If high-dose niacin (≥1g/day) is co-prescribed with statins, monitor for myopathy symptoms (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine). Obtain baseline and periodic CK levels. Avoid high-intensity statin doses in this combination.

thyroid_medications.label: Thyroid Medications
actions: Coordinate iodine supplements with thyroid medication management.
Separate calcium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours to prevent absorption interference.
Separate chromium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
Separate iron supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
Separate magnesium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
Separate zinc supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.

antihypertensives.label: Antihypertensives
actions: Monitor BP when supplementing alongside antihypertensives.
Separate green tea extract from beta-blocker dosing by at least 2-3 hours.
Separate iron supplement from methyldopa or captopril by at least 2 hours.
